6
Chapter 6  ·  Statistical Inference

Linear Regression:
A Complete Reference

From conditional expectation and geometric projection through inference, diagnostics, transformations, robust methods, and model selection — a rigorous, comprehensive treatment of the full regression pipeline.

Learning Objectives

  • Formulate regression as the problem of estimating a conditional expectation and prove that it uniquely minimizes mean squared prediction error over all measurable functions.
  • Decompose expected prediction error into irreducible noise, bias, and variance; interpret the bias–variance trade-off graphically and analytically.
  • Derive the OLS estimator as an orthogonal projection onto the column space of the design matrix and prove the normal equations from first principles.
  • Derive closed-form OLS estimators for simple linear regression, prove unbiasedness and minimum variance, and state the Gauss–Markov theorem with all regularity conditions.
  • Construct $t$-tests, global and partial $F$-tests, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals under normality; interpret ANOVA decompositions for regression.
  • Execute the four standard diagnostic plots; compute standardized residuals, leverage, Cook's distance, and DFBETAS to identify violations and influential observations.
  • Detect multicollinearity via VIF and condition number; understand its consequences and apply appropriate remedies including ridge regression.
  • Apply log, Box–Cox, and variance-stabilizing transformations; fit weighted and generalized least squares for heteroscedastic and correlated errors.
  • Model nonlinearity via polynomial regression, natural cubic splines, and LOESS; encode categorical predictors and interactions correctly.
  • Apply AIC, BIC, cross-validation, and regularization paths for model selection; diagnose and prevent overfitting using the bias–variance framework.

Part I

Foundations

6.1 Regression as Conditional Expectation

At its heart, regression answers a deceptively simple question: given that I observe $X = x$, what is my best prediction for $Y$? Before imposing any parametric structure — before writing down a single equation — we should understand what "best" means and what the universally optimal answer is. The answer, requiring no linearity assumptions whatsoever, is the conditional expectation $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]$. This function is the unique minimizer of mean squared prediction error over all measurable predictors, and every parametric regression model is an attempt to approximate it from finite data.

Understanding this foundation transforms how we read regression output. A regression model does not claim that $y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$ exactly — it claims that the conditional mean $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]$ is approximately $\beta_0 + \beta_1 x$. The scatter of points around the line is genuine, irreducible variation in $Y$ that no predictor can remove. This section establishes these facts rigorously before any parameters are introduced.

6.1.1 The Regression Function

Let $(X, Y)$ be a jointly distributed random vector with $\mathbb{E}[Y^2] < \infty$. We seek the function $f^*$ that most accurately predicts $Y$ from $X$ in the sense of minimizing expected squared error. There is no constraint on the functional form of $f^*$ — it can be any measurable function.

Definition · Regression Function

The regression function of $Y$ on $X$ is $$\mu(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x],$$ defined for each $x$ in the support of $X$. It is the unique (almost surely) minimizer of the mean squared prediction error: $$\mu = \operatorname*{arg\,min}_{f \,\text{measurable}} \mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y - f(X))^2\bigr].$$ The minimum value, $\mathbb{E}[(Y - \mu(X))^2] = \mathbb{E}[\text{Var}(Y \mid X)]$, is called the irreducible error — it represents the inherent randomness of $Y$ not explained by $X$, regardless of how well we estimate $\mu$.

Theorem · Optimality of Conditional Expectation

For any measurable function $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$: $$\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y - f(X))^2\bigr] = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y - \mu(X))^2\bigr]}_{\text{irreducible}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\bigl[(\mu(X) - f(X))^2\bigr]}_{\geq\, 0},$$ with equality in the second term if and only if $f(x) = \mu(x)$ for almost all $x$ in the support of $X$.

Proof

Write the prediction error as $Y - f(X) = [Y - \mu(X)] + [\mu(X) - f(X)]$. Squaring and taking expectations: $$\mathbb{E}[(Y - f(X))^2] = \mathbb{E}[(Y - \mu(X))^2] + 2\,\mathbb{E}[(Y - \mu(X))(\mu(X) - f(X))] + \mathbb{E}[(\mu(X) - f(X))^2].$$ The cross term vanishes by the tower property of conditional expectation: $$\mathbb{E}[(Y - \mu(X))(\mu(X) - f(X))] = \mathbb{E}\Bigl[(\mu(X) - f(X))\underbrace{\mathbb{E}[Y - \mu(X) \mid X]}_{= \mu(X) - \mu(X) = 0}\Bigr] = 0.$$ Since $\mathbb{E}[(\mu(X) - f(X))^2] \geq 0$ with equality iff $f = \mu$ a.s., the result follows. □

This theorem is profound: it tells us that no predictor of $Y$ from $X$ — linear, nonlinear, neural network, or otherwise — can achieve expected squared error below the irreducible noise level $\mathbb{E}[\text{Var}(Y \mid X)]$. The regression function $\mu(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]$ is the Platonic ideal predictor, and all practical methods are attempts to estimate it.

6.1.2 Parametric vs. Nonparametric Regression

The regression function $\mu(x)$ is an arbitrary function and, in principle, must be estimated nonparametrically from data. Nonparametric methods (kernel smoothers, splines, LOESS) place no constraints on the shape of $\mu$. Linear regression restricts $\mu(x)$ to the class of affine functions: $\mu(x;\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$. This is a strong assumption, but it is justified in three complementary ways:

  • Physical or domain theory: Many relationships are genuinely linear over the observed range (e.g., Hooke's law, Beer-Lambert law in spectrophotometry, many economic relationships in logs).
  • Local approximation: Taylor's theorem guarantees that any smooth function $\mu$ is approximately affine near any point. Linear regression captures the dominant term of the local approximation.
  • Statistical efficiency: When the linear model is correct, OLS achieves the Cramér–Rao lower bound among unbiased estimators. Nonparametric methods pay a statistical price for their flexibility.
Intuition · What Regression Estimates

A regression line does not say "individual $i$ with $X = x$ has $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$." It says "among all individuals with $X = x$, the average $Y$ is $\beta_0 + \beta_1 x$." The scatter of individual observations around this line is real, inherent variation — not measurement error or model failure. Regression describes the center of a conditional distribution, not a deterministic mechanism. This distinction is crucial for correct interpretation of coefficients and for understanding why prediction intervals (which account for individual scatter) are always wider than confidence intervals for the mean (Section 6.5).

6.1.3 Supervised Learning and Bias–Variance Decomposition

In the language of machine learning, regression is a supervised learning problem: we observe $n$ training pairs $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ drawn i.i.d. from a joint distribution $P_{XY}$, and wish to learn a function $\hat{f}$ that predicts $Y$ for new observations from the same distribution. The expected generalization error of any estimator $\hat{f}$ decomposes into three components — a result of fundamental importance.

Theorem · Bias–Variance Decomposition

Let $x_0$ be a fixed test point, $Y_0 = \mu(x_0) + \varepsilon_0$ a new observation with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_0] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_0) = \sigma^2$. For any estimator $\hat{f}$ trained on $n$ samples from $P_{XY}$, the expected test MSE decomposes as: $$\mathbb{E}\bigl[(Y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0))^2\bigr] = \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\substack{\text{Irreducible}\\\text{noise}}} + \underbrace{\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)] - \mu(x_0)\bigr)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f})} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0))}_{\text{Variance}(\hat{f})},$$ where $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(Y_0 \mid X = x_0)$, and all expectations over $\hat{f}$ are with respect to random training samples of size $n$.

Proof

Let $\bar{f} = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)]$ (expectation over training sets). Decompose: $$Y_0 - \hat{f}(x_0) = \underbrace{(Y_0 - \mu(x_0))}_{\varepsilon_0} + \underbrace{(\mu(x_0) - \bar{f})}_{-\text{Bias}} + \underbrace{(\bar{f} - \hat{f}(x_0))}_{\text{zero-mean}}.$$ Since $\varepsilon_0$ is independent of training data, $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_0 \cdot g(\hat{f})] = \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_0]\mathbb{E}[g(\hat{f})] = 0$ for any function $g$. The term $(\mu - \bar{f})$ is a constant with respect to training data, and $(\bar{f} - \hat{f})$ has mean zero. All three cross products vanish, giving: $$\mathbb{E}[(Y_0 - \hat{f})^2] = \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_0^2] + (\mu - \bar{f})^2 + \mathbb{E}[(\bar{f} - \hat{f})^2] = \sigma^2 + \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}(\hat{f}). \qquad$$ □

The bias–variance decomposition reveals a fundamental trade-off. Simple models (e.g., the intercept-only model) are highly biased — they impose too much structure and systematically miss the true $\mu$ — but have low variance since they depend little on which particular training sample was observed. Complex models (high-degree polynomials, deep neural networks with many parameters) can achieve low bias by closely approximating $\mu$, but have high variance — small changes in the training data produce large changes in $\hat{f}$. The optimal model minimizes the sum of all three terms, and this optimum is not at either extreme.

Figure 6.1. Left: 300 observations with the true regression function $\mathbb{E}[Y|X=x]=2+0.8x$ (blue line). Vertical density fills at $x=2$ (green), $x=5$ (gold), and $x=8$ (red) show the conditional distributions $Y|X=x$ — each a $\mathcal{N}(2+0.8x,\,1.8^2)$ curve. The regression line passes through the mean of each conditional distribution, while the spread represents irreducible noise $\sigma^2=3.24$. Right: The bias–variance trade-off as a function of model complexity. Bias$^2$ (blue) decreases monotonically with complexity; variance (gold) increases; total MSE (navy) is minimized at the dashed red vertical line — neither the simplest nor most complex model is optimal.
Example · Computing the Bias–Variance Decomposition for OLS

Setup: Suppose the true model is $Y = \sin(X) + \varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, 0.25)$, and we fit a simple linear regression $\hat{f}(x) = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x$ on $n = 30$ observations with $X \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \text{Uniform}(0, \pi)$.

Bias: The expected OLS fit is $\bar{f}(x) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_0] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_1]x$, which is the best linear approximation to $\sin(x)$ over $[0, \pi]$. At $x_0 = \pi/4$, $\sin(\pi/4) \approx 0.707$ while the best linear fit gives approximately $0.626$, so $\text{Bias} \approx 0.707 - 0.626 = 0.081$ and $\text{Bias}^2 \approx 0.0066$.

Variance: $\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0)) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{x}_0^\top (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{x}_0 \approx 0.25/30 \cdot (1 + \text{leverage factor}) \approx 0.009$.

Irreducible noise: $\sigma^2 = 0.25$.

Total expected test MSE: $\approx 0.25 + 0.0066 + 0.009 = 0.266$. The linear model has small but nonzero bias because $\sin(x)$ is not linear; most of the error is irreducible noise.

Data Science Application · Regularization as Bias–Variance Control

In modern machine learning, the bias–variance trade-off is not just descriptive — it is actionable. Ridge regression (Section 6.22) adds a penalty $\lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2$ that deliberately biases $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ toward zero, trading increased bias for reduced variance. Deep learning addresses the trade-off differently: extremely large models (low bias) are paired with early stopping, dropout, and weight decay (variance reduction), achieving low total MSE despite having more parameters than observations. The classical "sweet spot" picture from Figure 6.1 is the organizing principle behind all of these techniques.

6.2 Geometry of Least Squares

Ordinary least squares has a beautiful geometric interpretation that makes many of its properties immediately transparent. Rather than thinking of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ as the minimizer of a sum of squared residuals — a purely algebraic statement — we can think of the fitted value vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ as the orthogonal projection of the response vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ onto the column space of the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$. This single geometric fact unifies the normal equations, the ANOVA identity, the hat matrix, the unbiasedness proof, and the Gauss–Markov theorem.

The central objects are: the response vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^n$; the column space $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X}) = \{\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\gamma} : \boldsymbol{\gamma} \in \mathbb{R}^p\}$, a $p$-dimensional linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$; the projection matrix $\mathbf{H}$; and the residual vector $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}$. When $n > p$ (more observations than parameters), $\mathbf{y}$ will not in general lie in $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$, so we find the closest point in $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$ to $\mathbf{y}$ — the projection.

6.2.1 Column Space and the Hat Matrix

Definition · Hat Matrix (Orthogonal Projector)

Given a full-rank $n \times p$ design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ (rank $p$), the hat matrix (or projection matrix) is $$\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top.$$ It is the orthogonal projector onto $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$: $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$. Its complement $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{I}_n - \mathbf{H}$ projects onto the orthogonal complement $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})^\perp$, giving residuals $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}$.

Theorem · Properties of $\mathbf{H}$

The hat matrix satisfies all of the following:

  1. $\mathbf{H}^2 = \mathbf{H}$  (idempotent)
  2. $\mathbf{H}^\top = \mathbf{H}$  (symmetric)
  3. $\operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p$
  4. All eigenvalues of $\mathbf{H}$ are $0$ or $1$
  5. $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}$  (projects column space to itself)
  6. $\mathbf{M}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{0}$  (projection and annihilator are orthogonal)
  7. $0 \leq h_{ii} \leq 1$ for all $i$, where $h_{ii} = [\mathbf{H}]_{ii}$ is the leverage of observation $i$

Proof

Idempotency: $\mathbf{H}^2 = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\underbrace{\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}}_{=\mathbf{I}}\mathbf{X}^\top = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top = \mathbf{H}$. Symmetry: $\mathbf{H}^\top = [\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top]^\top = \mathbf{X}[(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}]^\top\mathbf{X}^\top = \mathbf{H}$ since $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})$ is symmetric positive definite. Rank: For any idempotent symmetric matrix, eigenvalues are in $\{0,1\}$ (from $\lambda^2 = \lambda$), so $\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = \operatorname{rank}(\mathbf{H}) = p$ since $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{H}) = \mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$ has dimension $p$. Property 5: $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}$. Leverage bounds: $h_{ii} = \|\mathbf{H}_{i\cdot}\|^2 \geq 0$; idempotency gives $h_{ii} = h_{ii}^2 + \sum_{j\neq i}h_{ij}^2 \leq 1$. □

6.2.2 The Normal Equations

The OLS minimization problem has a direct geometric solution. The minimum of $\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2$ over all $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is achieved when the residual $\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is perpendicular to every vector in $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$ — that is, perpendicular to every column of $\mathbf{X}$. This orthogonality requirement, $\mathbf{X}^\top(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \mathbf{0}$, is precisely the normal equations.

Normal Equations
$$(\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$
Theorem · OLS as Projection; Uniqueness

If $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank $p \leq n$, the unique minimizer of $\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2$ over $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^p$ is $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$. The minimum value is $\|\mathbf{e}\|^2 = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}$.

Proof

For any $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^p$, decompose: $$\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \underbrace{(\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y})}_{\mathbf{e}\,\in\,\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})^\perp} + \underbrace{\mathbf{X}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta})}_{\in\,\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})}.$$ These two components are orthogonal (one in $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$, one in $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})^\perp$), so by Pythagoras: $$\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{X}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta})\|^2 \geq \|\mathbf{e}\|^2,$$ with equality iff $\boldsymbol{\beta} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$. Uniqueness follows since $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank, so $\|\mathbf{X}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta})\|^2 = 0$ only when $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \boldsymbol{\beta}$. □

6.2.3 Orthogonality Conditions and the ANOVA Identity

The residual vector $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}$ is orthogonal to every column of $\mathbf{X}$: $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$. When $\mathbf{X}$ contains a column of ones (intercept), this has immediate consequences:

  • $\mathbf{1}^\top\mathbf{e} = 0$: OLS residuals always sum to zero.
  • $\mathbf{x}_j^\top\mathbf{e} = 0$ for each predictor column $\mathbf{x}_j$: residuals are uncorrelated with every predictor.
  • $\hat{\mathbf{y}}^\top\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{H}\mathbf{y})^\top(\mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{H}\mathbf{M}\mathbf{y} = 0$: fitted values are uncorrelated with residuals.

These orthogonality conditions yield the fundamental ANOVA identity by a Pythagorean argument in $\mathbb{R}^n$.

Example · ANOVA Identity as Pythagoras in $\mathbb{R}^n$

Setup: Show that SST = SSR + SSE holds in any regression with an intercept, as a consequence of geometric orthogonality.

Derivation: Center the data: $\mathbf{y} - \bar{y}\mathbf{1} = (\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \bar{y}\mathbf{1}) + \mathbf{e}$. Since an intercept is included, $\bar{y}\mathbf{1} \in \mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$, so $\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \bar{y}\mathbf{1} \in \mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$. The residual $\mathbf{e} \perp \mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$. Therefore the two components on the right are orthogonal. By the Pythagorean theorem in $\mathbb{R}^n$: $$\|\mathbf{y} - \bar{y}\mathbf{1}\|^2 = \|\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \bar{y}\mathbf{1}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \underbrace{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}_{\text{SST}} = \underbrace{\sum(\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2}_{\text{SSR}} + \underbrace{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}_{\text{SSE}}.$$

Meaning: This is not an algebraic coincidence — it is the Pythagorean theorem applied to vectors in $n$-dimensional Euclidean space. The total variation SST is decomposed into the variation explained by the model (SSR, the length$^2$ of the projection) and the variation unexplained (SSE, the length$^2$ of the residual perpendicular to the column space). The geometry makes the decomposition exact and transparent.

Figure 6.2. Geometric picture of OLS. The response vector $\mathbf{y}$ (navy arrow) is projected orthogonally onto the column space $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$ (blue shaded region), yielding the fitted value vector $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$ (blue arrow). The residual $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ (red arrow) is perpendicular to the column space, as shown by the right-angle marker. This orthogonality is the geometric statement of the normal equations $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}$. The ANOVA decomposition SST = SSR + SSE follows from Pythagoras applied to the triangle formed by these three vectors.
Intuition · Over- and Underdetermined Systems

When $p > n$ (more parameters than observations), $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ is singular — the normal equations have infinitely many solutions. There is always a solution $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ that achieves SSE = 0 (perfect fit), but it is not unique and generalizes poorly. Regularization (Section 6.22) resolves this by selecting the unique minimum-norm solution. When $n = p$ (exactly), $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{I}$, $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{y}$ exactly, and all residuals are zero — interpolation, not regression. Useful inference requires $n \gg p$.

Data Science Application · QR Decomposition for Numerical Stability

Computing $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ directly is numerically dangerous: forming $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ squares the condition number $\kappa(\mathbf{X})$, so a moderately ill-conditioned $\mathbf{X}$ with $\kappa = 1000$ produces a nearly singular $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$ with $\kappa^2 = 10^6$. The QR decomposition $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{R}$ (thin QR, $\mathbf{Q}^\top\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{I}_p$) gives $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{Q}^\top$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{Q}^\top\mathbf{y}$ — preserving the condition number of $\mathbf{X}$. This is what numpy.linalg.lstsq, R's lm(), and sklearn.linear_model.LinearRegression use internally. For severely rank-deficient problems, SVD replaces QR.

Part II

Simple Linear Regression

6.3 Model and Estimation

Simple linear regression — one predictor, one response — is the entry point for the full regression framework. The simplicity of the scalar setting allows us to derive closed-form estimators by differentiating a sum of squares and to build geometric intuition that carries over directly to multiple regression. In Section 6.8 we re-derive all results using matrix notation, which handles the general case elegantly; here the scalar formulas themselves build understanding of what the estimators actually measure.

The model asserts a linear form for the conditional mean $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]$ and specifies a probabilistic error structure. These two components — the structural and stochastic parts — must be kept conceptually distinct. The structural part determines what coefficients mean; the stochastic part determines how to estimate them and what their sampling properties are.

6.3.1 Model Specification

Definition · Simple Linear Regression Model

We observe $n$ pairs $(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$ satisfying $$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i, \qquad i = 1, \ldots, n,$$ where:

  • $\beta_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ is the intercept — the expected value of $Y$ when $X = 0$.
  • $\beta_1 \in \mathbb{R}$ is the slope — the expected change in $Y$ per unit increase in $X$.
  • $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ are fixed (non-random) or conditioned upon — we treat them as known constants.
  • $\varepsilon_1, \ldots, \varepsilon_n \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} (0, \sigma^2)$ — zero-mean, constant-variance, uncorrelated errors.
The additional assumption $\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ is required only for exact finite-sample inference (Section 6.5). The Gauss–Markov theorem (Section 6.4) holds without normality.

Notice what the model does and does not assume. It assumes $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ — a linear conditional mean — but places no constraint on the marginal distribution of $X$. It assumes homoscedasticity ($\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$) and uncorrelated errors, but not normality. These assumptions will be tested in Section 6.6 and relaxed in Sections 6.12, 6.15, and 6.17.

6.3.2 OLS Derivation and Closed-Form Estimators

The ordinary least squares (OLS) criterion is to find the values of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ that minimize the residual sum of squares: $$Q(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^n (y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i)^2.$$ This is a quadratic function of $(\beta_0, \beta_1)$, strictly convex (since $S_{xx} > 0$ when not all $x_i$ are equal), so it has a unique global minimum found by setting partial derivatives to zero.

Theorem · Closed-Form OLS Estimators in Simple Regression

Let $\bar{x} = n^{-1}\sum x_i$, $\bar{y} = n^{-1}\sum y_i$, $S_{xx} = \sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\bar{x})^2$, $S_{xy} = \sum_{i=1}^n(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$, and $S_{yy} = \sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\bar{y})^2$. Then the unique minimizers of $Q$ are: $$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}, \qquad \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}.$$ The minimum residual sum of squares is $\text{SSE} = S_{yy} - \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy}$.

Proof

First-order conditions: Setting $\partial Q / \partial \beta_0 = 0$: $$-2\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0 \implies n\beta_0 + \beta_1\sum x_i = \sum y_i \implies \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}.$$ Setting $\partial Q / \partial \beta_1 = 0$: $$-2\sum_{i=1}^n x_i(y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x_i) = 0.$$ Substituting $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}$: the $i$-th term becomes $x_i(y_i - \bar{y} + \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x} - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i) = x_i(y_i - \bar{y}) - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i(x_i - \bar{x})$. Summing: $\sum x_i(y_i - \bar{y}) = \sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y}) = S_{xy}$ and $\sum x_i(x_i-\bar{x}) = S_{xx}$. Thus $\hat{\beta}_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}$. SSE formula: $\text{SSE} = S_{yy} - 2\hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy} + \hat{\beta}_1^2 S_{xx} = S_{yy} - \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy}$. □

OLS Slope and Intercept
$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}} = r_{xy}\frac{s_y}{s_x}, \qquad \hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}, \qquad \text{SSE} = S_{yy} - \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy}$$

The formula $\hat{\beta}_1 = r_{xy}(s_y/s_x)$ expresses the slope as the sample correlation scaled by the ratio of standard deviations. When both variables are standardized ($s_x = s_y = 1$), the OLS slope equals the sample correlation $r_{xy}$. This relationship connects the regression framework to correlation analysis and will be revisited when discussing $R^2$ in Section 6.7.

6.3.3 Coefficient Interpretation and Regression to the Mean

The slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ has a clear interpretation: a one-unit increase in $x$ is associated with a $\hat{\beta}_1$-unit change in the predicted value of $y$, holding all else equal. The intercept $\hat{\beta}_0$ is the predicted $y$ when $x = 0$ — which may or may not be a meaningful quantity depending on whether $x = 0$ is in or near the observed data range.

Intuition · Regression to the Mean

Francis Galton observed in 1886 that tall fathers tend to have sons who are tall but not as tall as their fathers (relative to their generation's mean), and short fathers tend to have sons who are short but not as short. He called this "regression to mediocrity." The formula $\hat{\beta}_1 = r_{xy}(s_y/s_x)$ explains why: in standardized units, the slope is $r_{xy} \in [-1, 1]$. An observation $k$ standard deviations above $\bar{x}$ predicts a response only $|r_{xy}| \cdot k$ standard deviations from $\bar{y}$ — closer to the mean. The imperfect correlation forces predicted extreme values back toward the center. This is a mathematical consequence of imperfect correlation, not a physical law governing any particular system over time.

Example · Calibration Curve in Analytical Chemistry

Setup: A spectrophotometer is calibrated by measuring absorbance $y$ at known concentrations $x \in \{0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0\}$ mg/mL ($n = 7$). We compute: $\bar{x} = 0.871$, $\bar{y} = 0.433$, $S_{xx} = 2.694$, $S_{xy} = 1.347$, $S_{yy} = 0.680$.

Estimation: $$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{1.347}{2.694} = 0.500 \text{ abs./(mg/mL)}, \qquad \hat{\beta}_0 = 0.433 - 0.500 \times 0.871 = -0.003 \approx 0.$$ The intercept near zero is physically sensible: zero concentration should give near-zero absorbance (Beer's law).

Fitted values and residuals: At $x = 1.0$: $\hat{y} = -0.003 + 0.500 \times 1.0 = 0.497$. $\text{SSE} = 0.680 - 0.500 \times 1.347 = 0.006$, so $s = \sqrt{0.006/5} = 0.035$ absorbance units.

Interpretation: The calibration curve is nearly perfectly linear (SSE is tiny relative to SST = 0.680). The slope of 0.500 is the Beer-Lambert coefficient. The model can now be inverted to estimate unknown concentrations from observed absorbances.

Warning · Correlation Is Not Causation

The OLS slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ estimates the linear association between $X$ and $Y$, not a causal effect. When the error $\varepsilon_i$ is correlated with $x_i$ — due to omitted confounders, reverse causation, or selection bias — $\hat{\beta}_1$ is a biased estimator of the causal effect $\partial\mathbb{E}[Y \mid \text{do}(X = x)] / \partial x$. The most persuasive evidence for causality comes from randomized experiments (where $X$ is assigned independently of $\varepsilon$ by design) or from natural experiments with instrumental variables. Observational regression coefficients should be interpreted as predictive associations unless a careful causal argument accompanies them.

6.4 Statistical Properties of OLS

The estimators $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ are functions of the random responses $y_1, \ldots, y_n$ (through the errors $\varepsilon_i$) and thus are themselves random variables with their own distributions. Understanding these sampling distributions is the foundation for hypothesis testing and confidence intervals in Section 6.5. The central result of this section — the Gauss–Markov theorem — establishes OLS as the optimal estimator within the class of linear unbiased estimators, under conditions that do not require normality of the errors.

6.4.1 Unbiasedness

Theorem · Unbiasedness of OLS Estimators

Under the simple linear model with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_i] = 0$ for all $i$: $$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_0] = \beta_0, \qquad \mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_1] = \beta_1.$$

Proof

Write $\hat{\beta}_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx} = \sum_{i=1}^n c_i y_i$ where $c_i = (x_i - \bar{x})/S_{xx}$. Substituting $y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$: $$\hat{\beta}_1 = \beta_0\underbrace{\sum c_i}_{=0} + \beta_1\underbrace{\sum c_i x_i}_{=1} + \underbrace{\sum c_i \varepsilon_i}_{\text{mean zero}} = \beta_1 + \sum c_i\varepsilon_i.$$ The identities hold because $\sum c_i = \sum(x_i - \bar{x})/S_{xx} = 0$ and $\sum c_i x_i = \sum(x_i - \bar{x})x_i/S_{xx} = S_{xx}/S_{xx} = 1$. Therefore $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_1] = \beta_1 + \sum c_i \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_i] = \beta_1$. For $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}$: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_0] = \mathbb{E}[\bar{y}] - \bar{x}\mathbb{E}[\hat{\beta}_1] = (\beta_0 + \beta_1\bar{x}) - \beta_1\bar{x} = \beta_0$. □

6.4.2 Variance of the Estimators

Theorem · Variance of OLS Estimators

Under $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ and $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$: $$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}}, \qquad \text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_0) = \sigma^2\!\left(\frac{1}{n} + \frac{\bar{x}^2}{S_{xx}}\right), \qquad \text{Cov}(\hat{\beta}_0, \hat{\beta}_1) = -\frac{\sigma^2\bar{x}}{S_{xx}}.$$

Proof

From the proof above, $\hat{\beta}_1 = \beta_1 + \sum c_i\varepsilon_i$ with $c_i = (x_i - \bar{x})/S_{xx}$. Since errors are uncorrelated: $$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sum c_i^2\,\sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}^2}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}}.$$ For $\hat{\beta}_0$: write $\hat{\beta}_0 = \sum d_i y_i$ where $d_i = 1/n - c_i\bar{x}$. Then $\sum d_i^2 = 1/n + \bar{x}^2/S_{xx}$ (by expanding and using $\sum c_i = 0$). So $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_0) = \sigma^2\sum d_i^2$. The covariance: $\text{Cov}(\hat{\beta}_0, \hat{\beta}_1) = \sigma^2\sum d_i c_i = \sigma^2(0 - \bar{x}/S_{xx}) = -\sigma^2\bar{x}/S_{xx}$. □

The variance formulas have natural interpretations. $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sigma^2/S_{xx}$ decreases when (i) the error variance $\sigma^2$ decreases (less noise), or (ii) $S_{xx} = \sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ increases (more spread in the covariate values). This provides guidance for experimental design: spreading the design points $x_i$ over a wide range gives the most precise slope estimate. For fixed $S_{xx}$, the optimal two-point design concentrates all observations at the two extreme values of the design region.

6.4.3 The Gauss–Markov Theorem

The Gauss–Markov theorem provides the strongest possible justification for OLS: under conditions that do not require normality, OLS achieves the smallest possible variance among all estimators that are both linear in the $y_i$ and unbiased. No other such estimator can do better. "BLUE" stands for Best Linear Unbiased Estimator.

Theorem · Gauss–Markov (Simple Regression)

Suppose $y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$ with (GM1) $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_i] = 0$, (GM2) $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2 < \infty$ for all $i$ (homoscedasticity), and (GM3) $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ (uncorrelated errors). Then among all estimators $\tilde{\beta}_1 = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i y_i$ that are (a) linear in $\{y_i\}$ and (b) unbiased ($\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\beta}_1] = \beta_1$ for all $\beta_0, \beta_1, \sigma^2$), the OLS estimator $\hat{\beta}_1$ has the smallest variance: $$\text{Var}(\tilde{\beta}_1) \geq \text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \frac{\sigma^2}{S_{xx}}.$$ The analogous result holds for $\hat{\beta}_0$ and for any linear combination $c_0\beta_0 + c_1\beta_1$.

Proof

Let $\tilde{\beta}_1 = \sum a_i y_i$ be linear and unbiased. Unbiasedness requires $\mathbb{E}[\tilde{\beta}_1] = \beta_0\sum a_i + \beta_1\sum a_i x_i = \beta_1$ for all $\beta_0, \beta_1$, which forces $\sum a_i = 0$ and $\sum a_i x_i = 1$. Write $a_i = c_i + d_i$ where $c_i = (x_i - \bar{x})/S_{xx}$ are the OLS weights. The unbiasedness constraints become $\sum d_i = 0$ and $\sum d_i x_i = 0$ (i.e., $\sum d_i(x_i - \bar{x}) = 0$). Under (GM2)–(GM3): $$\text{Var}(\tilde{\beta}_1) = \sigma^2\sum a_i^2 = \sigma^2\!\left(\sum c_i^2 + 2\sum c_i d_i + \sum d_i^2\right).$$ The cross term: $\sum c_i d_i = S_{xx}^{-1}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})d_i = S_{xx}^{-1}(\sum x_i d_i - \bar{x}\sum d_i) = S_{xx}^{-1}(1 \cdot 0 - \bar{x} \cdot 0) = 0$. Therefore $\text{Var}(\tilde{\beta}_1) = \sigma^2/S_{xx} + \sigma^2\sum d_i^2 \geq \sigma^2/S_{xx} = \text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1)$, with equality iff $d_i = 0$ for all $i$ (i.e., $\tilde{\beta}_1 = \hat{\beta}_1$). □

6.4.4 Estimating the Error Variance

The parameters $\beta_0, \beta_1$ describe the signal; $\sigma^2$ describes the noise. Since $\sigma^2$ is unknown, we must estimate it from the residuals. The correct estimate requires a degrees-of-freedom correction that accounts for the two parameters used in fitting.

Definition · Residual Mean Square

The residual mean square (mean squared error) is: $$s^2 = \hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{\text{SSE}}{n-2} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{n-2} = \frac{S_{yy} - \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy}}{n-2}.$$ This is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$: $\mathbb{E}[s^2] = \sigma^2$. Under normality, $(n-2)s^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-2}$, independently of $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$.

Intuition · Why Divide by $n - 2$?

There are $n$ residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$, but they satisfy exactly two linear constraints: $\sum e_i = 0$ (from the intercept normal equation) and $\sum x_i e_i = 0$ (from the slope normal equation). So only $n - 2$ of the residuals are "free" — they span an $(n-2)$-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$. Dividing by $n$ instead of $n - 2$ underestimates $\sigma^2$ systematically: the residuals are forced to be smaller than the true errors because the fitted line passes through the data. The $n - 2$ correction removes this bias. In general, fitting $p$ parameters requires dividing by $n - p$.

Figure 6.3. Sampling distributions of $\hat{\beta}_0$ (left) and $\hat{\beta}_1$ (right) from $N = 3000$ Monte Carlo simulations. True model: $y_i = 3 + 1.5x_i + \varepsilon_i$ with $\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, 4)$, $n = 30$, $x_i \in [0, 10]$ equally spaced. Blue histograms show the empirical sampling distributions; gold curves show the theoretical normal density (exact under Gaussian errors, approximate by CLT otherwise). Dashed red lines mark true parameter values; dotted green lines mark simulation means — nearly identical to true values, confirming unbiasedness. The widths confirm the theoretical variance formulas.
Data Science Application · When Gauss–Markov Conditions Fail

In practice the three Gauss–Markov conditions frequently fail: (GM2) is violated when response variance increases with the mean (Poisson data, log-normal data, financial returns); (GM3) is violated in time series (autocorrelated residuals), spatial data (neighboring observations correlated), and clustered data (observations within a cluster correlated). When (GM2) fails, OLS is unbiased but inefficient — Weighted Least Squares (Section 6.17) is BLUE. When (GM3) fails, OLS remains unbiased but standard errors are wrong — heteroscedasticity- and autocorrelation-consistent (HAC) standard errors or Generalized Least Squares (Section 6.17) are required. Recognizing which condition is violated directs the appropriate remedy.

6.5 Inference in Simple Regression

Having derived unbiased estimators $\hat{\beta}_0$, $\hat{\beta}_1$ and characterized their sampling distributions, we can now address the central questions of statistical inference: Is there a significant linear relationship between $X$ and $Y$? What range of values for the true slope $\beta_1$ is consistent with the data? What is our uncertainty when predicting future observations? These questions are answered through hypothesis tests, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals — each demanding a slightly different probability statement.

The full inference machinery requires one additional assumption beyond the Gauss–Markov conditions: normality of errors, $\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. This gives the estimators exact (not approximate) $t$-distributions under finite samples. Without normality, the CLT guarantees that these procedures are asymptotically valid as $n \to \infty$, but for small samples the normality check matters — and the Q–Q plot (Section 6.6) is the primary tool for it.

6.5.1 Exact Sampling Distributions Under Normality

The three key distributional facts follow from the normality of $\varepsilon_i$ and the linearity of $\hat{\beta}_0$, $\hat{\beta}_1$ in the $y_i$. The independence between $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and $s^2$ — a subtler result that requires Cochran's theorem — is what converts the normal pivots into exact $t$-statistics.

Theorem · Exact Sampling Distributions Under Normality

Under $y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$ with $\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$ and fixed $x_i$:

  1. $\hat{\beta}_j \sim \mathcal{N}(\beta_j,\,\sigma^2 c_{jj})$ exactly, where $c_{00} = 1/n + \bar{x}^2/S_{xx}$ and $c_{11} = 1/S_{xx}$.
  2. $(n-2)s^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-2}$, independently of $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$.
  3. The pivot statistic $T_j = (\hat{\beta}_j - \beta_j)/(s\sqrt{c_{jj}}) \sim t_{n-2}$ exactly.
  4. $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ are jointly normal, with $\text{Cov}(\hat{\beta}_0, \hat{\beta}_1) = -\sigma^2\bar{x}/S_{xx}$.
Proof Sketch

Normality of estimators: $\hat{\beta}_1 = \sum c_i y_i$ is a linear combination of independent normal $y_i$, hence normal. Distribution of $s^2$: The residual vector $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}$ lies in the $(n-2)$-dimensional null space of $\mathbf{X}^\top$. Under normality, $\mathbf{M}\mathbf{y} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{M})$, and by Cochran's theorem $\|\mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}\|^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-2}$, independently of $\mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$. $t$-pivot: $T_1 = (\hat{\beta}_1 - \beta_1)/(\sigma/\sqrt{S_{xx}}) \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ and $(n-2)s^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-2}$ independently, so $T_1/(s/\sigma) = (\hat{\beta}_1 - \beta_1)/(s/\sqrt{S_{xx}}) \sim t_{n-2}$ by the definition of the $t$-distribution. □

The independence of $s^2$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is not obvious and deserves emphasis. In a one-sample setting, $\bar{y}$ and $s^2$ are independent under normality (a classical result). The regression analog says the fitted values $\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$ and residuals $\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{y}$ are independent, because $\mathbf{H}$ and $\mathbf{M}$ are orthogonal projectors ($\mathbf{H}\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{0}$) applied to a Gaussian vector.

6.5.2 Hypothesis Tests for Regression Coefficients

The most common hypothesis is $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$: the predictor $X$ has no linear effect on $Y$ at all. Under $H_0$, the slope is zero and the best prediction of $Y$ is just $\bar{y}$ regardless of $X$. Rejecting $H_0$ means the data support a non-zero linear relationship.

$t$-Test for $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_1^0$
$$t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1 - \beta_1^0}{\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1)} = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1 - \beta_1^0}{s/\sqrt{S_{xx}}} \sim t_{n-2} \text{ under } H_0$$

Two-sided: reject if $|t| > t_{n-2,\,\alpha/2}$.   One-sided ($H_1:\beta_1>0$): reject if $t > t_{n-2,\,\alpha}$.

The $p$-value for a two-sided test is $p = 2P(t_{n-2} > |t_\text{obs}|)$, and measures the probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as $t_\text{obs}$ if $H_0$ were true. A small $p$-value (e.g., $< 0.05$) means the data are hard to explain under $H_0$, not that the effect is large or practically important.

Example · Full $t$-Test Walkthrough — Advertising and Sales

Data: $n = 25$ markets. TV advertising spend $X$ (thousands \$) and weekly sales $Y$ (units). Summary statistics: $\bar{x} = 18.4$, $\bar{y} = 312.6$, $S_{xx} = 4{,}820$, $S_{xy} = 12{,}630$, $S_{yy} = 48{,}150$.

Step 1 — Estimate: $$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{12{,}630}{4{,}820} = 2.620 \text{ units/\$k}, \quad \hat{\beta}_0 = 312.6 - 2.620\times18.4 = 264.4.$$ Each additional \$1,000 in TV advertising is associated with 2.62 more units sold per week.

Step 2 — Estimate $\sigma^2$: $$\text{SSE} = S_{yy} - \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy} = 48{,}150 - 2.620\times12{,}630 = 14{,}033.$$ $$s^2 = \frac{14{,}033}{25 - 2} = 610.1, \quad s = 24.70 \text{ units}.$$

Step 3 — Standard error and $t$-statistic: $$\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_1) = \frac{s}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}} = \frac{24.70}{\sqrt{4{,}820}} = \frac{24.70}{69.43} = 0.356.$$ $$t = \frac{2.620 - 0}{0.356} = 7.36, \quad \text{df} = 23.$$

Step 4 — Decision: Critical value $t_{23,\,0.025} = 2.069$. Since $|7.36| \gg 2.069$, reject $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. The $p$-value is $p = 2P(t_{23} > 7.36) \approx 0.000001$.

Step 5 — 95% CI for $\beta_1$: $$2.620 \pm 2.069 \times 0.356 = 2.620 \pm 0.737 = (1.883,\; 3.357).$$ We are 95% confident the true effect of an extra \$1,000 in advertising lies between 1.88 and 3.36 additional units sold.

Step 6 — Test $H_0: \beta_0 = 0$ (does zero ad spend predict zero sales?): $$\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_0) = s\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{\bar{x}^2}{S_{xx}}} = 24.70\sqrt{\frac{1}{25} + \frac{18.4^2}{4{,}820}} = 24.70\times 0.270 = 6.67.$$ $$t_0 = 264.4/6.67 = 39.6 \Rightarrow \text{strongly reject } H_0:\beta_0=0. $$ At zero advertising, we predict ~264 units sold — the intercept is highly significant and represents baseline sales.

Intuition · What $p < 0.05$ Does and Does Not Mean

A significant $t$-test ($p < 0.05$) tells you the data are unlikely under the null — it does not tell you: (1) that the effect is large or practically meaningful (a slope of 0.0001 can be highly significant with $n = 10^6$); (2) that the model is correctly specified; (3) that the relationship is causal. Conversely, a non-significant result ($p > 0.05$) does not mean $\beta_1 = 0$ — it means there is insufficient evidence to reject $H_0$, which could reflect a small true effect, high noise, or small $n$. Always accompany a $p$-value with a confidence interval, which communicates both statistical and practical significance.

Figure 6.5a. Anatomy of the $t$-test for $H_0:\beta_1=0$ with $df=28$. Left: Two-sided test at $\alpha=0.05$: the red shaded rejection regions occupy 2.5% in each tail; the observed $t_\text{obs}=2.85$ (gold dashed) falls in the rejection region, giving $p=0.008$. Center: One-sided test ($H_1:\beta_1>0$): the entire 5% rejection region is in the upper tail, so the same $t_\text{obs}$ gives $p=0.004$ — half the two-sided value. Right: A non-significant result ($t_\text{obs}=1.45$, $p=0.158$): the test statistic falls in the blue "fail to reject" zone; the red areas (the $p$-value) are the probability of seeing something this extreme or more under $H_0$.

6.5.3 The ANOVA Decomposition

The ANOVA approach reframes the regression $t$-test as a comparison of two variance estimates. It partitions the total variation SST = $\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ into two orthogonal pieces — variation explained by the regression (SSR) and unexplained residual variation (SSE) — then tests whether SSR is large relative to SSE. The ratio of their mean squares follows an $F$-distribution under $H_0$.

Conceptually, the ANOVA decomposition answers: "How much of the scatter in $Y$ does the regression line account for?" If the regression explains most of the variation (SSR $\gg$ SSE), the slope is significant. If SSE $\approx$ SST (the line barely helps), $F$ is near 1 and we fail to reject $H_0$.

Figure 6.5c. Visual decomposition of SST = SSR + SSE for a small dataset ($n=12$). Left: Total variation SST — vertical lines show how far each $y_i$ deviates from the overall mean $\bar{y}$ (dashed). Center: Explained variation SSR — vertical lines show how far each fitted value $\hat{y}_i$ deviates from $\bar{y}$; this is the variation the regression "accounts for." Right: Residual variation SSE — vertical lines show how far each $y_i$ deviates from its fitted value $\hat{y}_i$; this is unexplained scatter. The three decompositions are exactly SST = SSR + SSE by Pythagoras (Section 6.2.3).
SourceSum of SquaresdfMean Square$F$-ratio$p$-value
Regression$\text{SSR} = \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy} = S_{xy}^2/S_{xx}$$1$$\text{MSR} = \text{SSR}/1$$F = \dfrac{\text{MSR}}{\text{MSE}} \sim F_{1,n-2}$$P(F_{1,n-2} \geq F_\text{obs})$
Residual$\text{SSE} = S_{yy} - \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy}$$n-2$$\text{MSE} = s^2 = \text{SSE}/(n-2)$
Total$\text{SST} = S_{yy}$$n-1$
Theorem · $F = t^2$ in Simple Regression

In simple linear regression, the global $F$-statistic and the $t$-statistic for $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$ satisfy $F = t^2$ exactly, and $F_{1,n-2} \overset{d}{=} t_{n-2}^2$ under $H_0$. The two tests are completely equivalent.

Proof

$F = \text{MSR}/\text{MSE} = (\hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy})/s^2 = \hat{\beta}_1^2 S_{xx}/s^2 = (\hat{\beta}_1/(s/\sqrt{S_{xx}}))^2 = t^2$. The equivalence $F_{1,\nu} \overset{d}{=} t_\nu^2$ follows from the fact that $t_\nu = Z/\sqrt{\chi^2_\nu/\nu}$ implies $t_\nu^2 = Z^2/(\chi^2_\nu/\nu) = \chi^2_1/(\chi^2_\nu/\nu) \sim F_{1,\nu}$. □

Example · Full ANOVA Table — Advertising Data

Continuing the advertising example: SST $= 48{,}150$, SSR $= 48{,}150 - 14{,}033 = 34{,}117$, SSE $= 14{,}033$.

SourceSSdfMS$F$$p$
Regression34,117134,11755.9<0.0001
Residual14,03323610.1—
Total48,15024——

$F = 55.9 = (7.36)^2 = t^2$ ✓. $R^2 = \text{SSR}/\text{SST} = 34{,}117/48{,}150 = 0.709$: TV advertising explains 71% of the variation in sales across markets.

6.5.4 Confidence Intervals for Regression Coefficients

A confidence interval captures the range of parameter values consistent with the data at a given confidence level. Unlike a hypothesis test (which gives a binary reject/not-reject), a CI communicates both the direction and the precision of our estimate. The interpretation is frequentist: if we repeated the experiment many times, 95% of such intervals would contain the true parameter.

Definition · $100(1-\alpha)\%$ Confidence Intervals

For the slope: $$\hat{\beta}_1 \pm t_{n-2,\,\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{S_{xx}}}.$$ For the intercept: $$\hat{\beta}_0 \pm t_{n-2,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{\bar{x}^2}{S_{xx}}}.$$ Both intervals have exact $100(1-\alpha)\%$ coverage under normality, regardless of $n$.

Intuition · Factors Controlling CI Width

The CI width for $\beta_1$ is $2t_{n-2,\alpha/2} \cdot s/\sqrt{S_{xx}}$. This shrinks when: (1) $s$ decreases — less noise means tighter estimates; (2) $S_{xx}$ increases — spreading the design points over a wider range extracts more information about the slope; (3) $n$ increases — through both reducing $s$ (more data reduces noise estimation uncertainty) and through the $t$-critical value approaching $z_{\alpha/2}$. The width does NOT shrink to zero for $\beta_0$ as $n \to \infty$ unless $\bar{x} \to 0$, because the intercept uncertainty is tied to $\bar{x}^2/S_{xx}$. Centering the predictors — replacing $x$ with $x - \bar{x}$ — sets $\bar{x}=0$ and minimizes the intercept standard error.

6.5.5 Confidence and Prediction Intervals for Responses

There are two conceptually different prediction tasks at a new covariate value $x^*$. The first is estimating the mean response $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x^*] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^*$ — a fixed parameter. The second is predicting a single new observation $Y^* = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^* + \varepsilon^*$ — a random variable. These lead to intervals of different widths because they quantify different sources of uncertainty.

Definition · Confidence Interval for Mean Response

A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ CI for $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x^*]$ is: $$\hat{y}^* \pm t_{n-2,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x^* - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}},$$ where $\hat{y}^* = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x^*$. This interval covers the population mean $\mu(x^*) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^*$. As $n \to \infty$, the interval collapses to the point $\mu(x^*)$.

Definition · Prediction Interval for a New Observation

A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ PI for a future observation $Y^* = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^* + \varepsilon^*$ is: $$\hat{y}^* \pm t_{n-2,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x^* - \bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}}.$$ The extra "1" under the square root represents $\text{Var}(\varepsilon^*)/\sigma^2$ — the irreducible noise of the new observation. As $n \to \infty$, the PI converges to $\hat{y}^* \pm z_{\alpha/2}\sigma$, not to a point.

Example · CI vs PI — Advertising Prediction at $x^* = 20$ (\$k)

Fitted value: $\hat{y}^* = 264.4 + 2.620 \times 20 = 316.8$ units.

CI for mean response: $$\text{SE}_{\text{CI}} = 24.70\sqrt{\frac{1}{25} + \frac{(20-18.4)^2}{4820}} = 24.70\sqrt{0.0400 + 0.00053} = 24.70 \times 0.2014 = 4.97.$$ $$\text{CI: } 316.8 \pm 2.069 \times 4.97 = 316.8 \pm 10.3 = (306.5,\; 327.1) \text{ units.}$$ We are 95% confident that markets spending \$20k on TV advertising average between 306.5 and 327.1 units sold.

PI for new market: $$\text{SE}_{\text{PI}} = 24.70\sqrt{1 + 0.0400 + 0.00053} = 24.70 \times 1.0202 = 25.20.$$ $$\text{PI: } 316.8 \pm 2.069 \times 25.20 = 316.8 \pm 52.1 = (264.7,\; 368.9) \text{ units.}$$ For a single new market at \$20k spend, we predict 265–369 units — a much wider range that reflects genuine market-to-market variation.

Key takeaway: The PI is about 5× wider than the CI here, reflecting that predicting an individual is fundamentally harder than estimating a population mean.

Warning · Extrapolation Amplifies Both Intervals

Both the CI and PI contain the term $(x^* - \bar{x})^2/S_{xx}$, which grows quadratically as $x^*$ moves away from $\bar{x}$. At $x^* = \bar{x}$, this term is zero — the interval is narrowest at the center of the data. At extreme $x^*$ values well outside the data range, both intervals blow up. This is a mathematical formalization of the statistical warning against extrapolation: the further you predict from the observed data, the less reliable the prediction. Some practitioners refuse to report PIs for $x^*$ values that give $h_{**} = 1/n + (x^* - \bar{x})^2/S_{xx} > 2p/n$, treating this as a "hidden extrapolation" flag.

Figure 6.5b. Left: OLS fit with 95% CI (blue band) and 95% PI (gold band) for $n=40$ observations. Both are narrowest at $\bar{x}$ (dotted vertical line) and widen with distance from the center. Double-headed arrows at $x=5$ and $x=12$ show the CI and PI half-widths at those points — the PI is always wider, and the difference grows near the edges. Right: Width of each interval as a function of $x^*$. The CI shrinks toward zero as $n\to\infty$; the PI converges to $2t^* s$ (the noise floor). The gold-shaded gap between the curves is the "excess uncertainty" from predicting an individual rather than a mean — driven entirely by the irreducible $\sigma^2$ term.
Data Science Application · When to Use CIs vs PIs

The choice between CI and PI depends entirely on what you are estimating. Use a CI when you want to characterize average behavior: "What is the expected salary of a software engineer with 5 years of experience?" Use a PI when you want to bound an individual outcome: "What salary should we budget for this specific candidate?" In healthcare, CIs are used for population-level treatment effects (clinical efficacy), while PIs are used for individual patient prognosis (clinical prediction). In manufacturing, PIs are used for process control — a new batch must meet specifications, not just average within them. Many practitioners incorrectly use CIs when PIs are appropriate, leading to dangerously overconfident individual predictions.

6.5.6 Simultaneous Inference and Bonferroni Correction

When performing multiple inference statements simultaneously (e.g., testing both $H_0:\beta_0=0$ and $H_0:\beta_1=0$, or constructing CIs for several fitted values), the family-wise error rate — the probability that at least one true $H_0$ is falsely rejected — exceeds $\alpha$ for any individual test. The Bonferroni correction addresses this by adjusting the significance level.

Definition · Bonferroni Correction and Working–Hotelling Band

When performing $m$ simultaneous tests, the Bonferroni correction uses level $\alpha/m$ for each test, ensuring family-wise error rate $\leq \alpha$. For simultaneous CIs at all values of $x^*$, the Working–Hotelling confidence band uses the critical value $\sqrt{2F_{2,n-2,\alpha}}$ instead of $t_{n-2,\alpha/2}$: $$\hat{y}^* \pm \sqrt{2F_{2,n-2,\alpha}} \cdot s\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} + \frac{(x^*-\bar{x})^2}{S_{xx}}},$$ which has 95% probability of containing the entire true line $\mu(x) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ for all $x$ simultaneously.

Intuition · Why Simultaneous Bands Are Wider

A 95% CI at a single $x^*$ has 5% chance of missing $\mu(x^*)$. If we compute CIs at 20 different $x^*$ values independently, we expect 1 of them to miss. The Working–Hotelling band is wide enough that the entire band — at every $x^*$ simultaneously — has only 5% chance of missing the true line anywhere. Since $2F_{2,n-2,0.05}^{1/2} > t_{n-2,0.025}$ always, the simultaneous band is wider than any pointwise CI. In practice, it is used when the goal is to characterize the regression line globally, e.g., for calibration purposes where predictions across the entire input range must be valid.

6.6 Model Fit and Diagnostics

Inferential procedures in regression — the $t$-tests, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals of Section 6.5 — are valid only when the model assumptions hold. If the true relationship is nonlinear, errors are heteroscedastic, or a few outliers distort the fit, the reported $p$-values and intervals may be meaningless. Diagnostic tools let us assess model assumptions from the data itself and identify the specific nature of any violations, pointing toward appropriate remedies.

The four standard diagnostic plots are the primary tools. Each addresses a specific assumption. Used together — never in isolation — they provide a comprehensive picture of model adequacy. We examine them in turn, understanding what good and bad patterns look like.

6.6.1 Standardized Residuals

Raw residuals $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ have different variances even under the homoscedastic model: $\text{Var}(e_i) = \sigma^2(1 - h_{ii})$, so observations with high leverage have smaller residual variance and can mask outliers. We correct for this by standardizing.

Definition · Standardized and Externally Studentized Residuals

The standardized residual is: $$r_i = \frac{e_i}{s\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}},$$ where $s^2 = \text{SSE}/(n-p)$ and $h_{ii} = [\mathbf{H}]_{ii}$ is the leverage of observation $i$. Under the correctly specified model, $r_i$ has approximately unit variance. The externally studentized residual (jackknife residual) is: $$t_i = \frac{e_i}{s_{(i)}\sqrt{1 - h_{ii}}},$$ where $s_{(i)}^2$ is the residual variance computed with observation $i$ deleted from the fit. Under normality, $t_i \sim t_{n-p-1}$ exactly, enabling formal outlier tests via Bonferroni correction.

6.6.2 The Four Diagnostic Plots

The four standard diagnostic plots examine: (1) residuals versus fitted values (linearity and homoscedasticity); (2) normal Q–Q plot of residuals (normality); (3) scale-location plot (homoscedasticity); and (4) residuals versus leverage (influence).

Example · Reading Each Diagnostic Plot

Residuals vs. Fitted: Under the correctly specified model, residuals should scatter randomly around zero with constant spread, showing no pattern. Systematic patterns indicate model misspecification: a U-shape (or inverted U) indicates a nonlinear term is missing; a funnel shape (wider at higher fitted values) indicates heteroscedasticity. The plot should look like random static.

Normal Q–Q plot: Orders the standardized residuals and plots them against the corresponding quantiles of $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Points should fall approximately on the diagonal reference line. An S-shaped departure (heavier than normal tails — points above the line at right, below at left) indicates heavy-tailed errors. A concave curve indicates right-skewed errors. Small deviations from normality are common and often inconsequential; large deviations invalidate exact $t/F$ inference.

Scale-Location: Plots $\sqrt{|r_i|}$ against fitted values. A horizontal band indicates homoscedasticity. A positive slope (or U-shape) indicates variance increasing with the fitted mean — the classic signature of multiplicative error structure, often remedied by a log transform of the response.

Residuals vs. Leverage: Plots $r_i$ against $h_{ii}$. The upper-right and lower-right quadrants (high leverage, large absolute residual) identify the most dangerous observations — those that have both the ability to influence the fit and the inclination to do so. Cook's distance contours (if shown) quantify this combined influence.

Figure 6.4. Standard diagnostic plots for a well-specified model ($n=80$, $y_i=2+1.2x_i+\varepsilon_i$, $\varepsilon_i\overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\mathcal{N}(0,2.25)$). Top left: Residuals vs. fitted — random scatter around zero with constant spread; no pattern (good). Top right: Q–Q plot — points closely follow the diagonal; normality approximately satisfied. Bottom left: Scale-location — roughly horizontal band confirms homoscedasticity. Bottom right: Residuals vs. leverage — all points inside the $2p/n$ leverage threshold (dashed gold); no extreme Cook's distances. This is the ideal picture; real data will typically show some deviations.
Warning · Residuals Are Not Errors

Even when the model is perfectly correct and $\varepsilon_i \overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, the fitted residuals $e_i$ are not i.i.d. Their covariance matrix is $\sigma^2\mathbf{M} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})$, not $\sigma^2\mathbf{I}$. In particular, $\text{Var}(e_i) = \sigma^2(1 - h_{ii})$, which varies across observations. High-leverage points have smaller residual variance — their $y_i$ is "pulled" toward $\hat{y}_i$ — which can mask outliers. This is why raw $e_i$ values should never be examined directly; always use standardized residuals $r_i$ that correct for the differing leverage values.

6.6.3 Autocorrelation in Residuals

When observations have a natural ordering (time series, spatial data), the independence assumption (GM3) is particularly important to check. The Durbin-Watson statistic tests for first-order autocorrelation in residuals:

Definition · Durbin-Watson Statistic

$$DW = \frac{\sum_{i=2}^n(e_i - e_{i-1})^2}{\sum_{i=1}^n e_i^2} \approx 2(1 - \hat{\rho}),$$ where $\hat{\rho}$ is the sample lag-1 autocorrelation of residuals. Values near 2 indicate no autocorrelation; $DW < 1.5$ suggests positive autocorrelation (adjacent residuals similar); $DW > 2.5$ suggests negative autocorrelation. The exact critical values depend on $n$ and $p$ (Durbin-Watson tables).

6.7 Goodness of Fit: $R^2$ and Adjusted $R^2$

Having fitted and diagnosed a model, we naturally ask: how well does it fit the data? The most widely reported measure is $R^2$, the coefficient of determination. Despite its near-universal use, $R^2$ is one of the most frequently misinterpreted statistics in applied work. This section defines it rigorously, establishes its relationship to the correlation coefficient and the ANOVA decomposition, and explains — with concrete examples — the many ways it can mislead.

6.7.1 Definition and Properties

Definition · Coefficient of Determination $R^2$

The coefficient of determination is: $$R^2 = \frac{\text{SSR}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}}{\text{SST}} = 1 - \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}.$$ When the model includes an intercept, $R^2 \in [0, 1]$, with $R^2 = 0$ meaning the model (slope) contributes nothing beyond predicting $\bar{y}$ for every observation, and $R^2 = 1$ meaning perfect fit with zero residuals.

Theorem · $R^2$ Equals Squared Correlation in Simple Regression

In simple linear regression with an intercept, $R^2 = r_{xy}^2$, where $r_{xy}$ is the sample Pearson correlation between $x$ and $y$. Equivalently, $R^2$ equals the squared correlation between the observed values $y_i$ and the fitted values $\hat{y}_i$.

Proof

$\text{SSR} = \hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy} = (S_{xy}/S_{xx}) \cdot S_{xy} = S_{xy}^2/S_{xx}$. Therefore: $$R^2 = \frac{S_{xy}^2/S_{xx}}{S_{yy}} = \frac{S_{xy}^2}{S_{xx} S_{yy}} = r_{xy}^2,$$ where the last equality uses the definition $r_{xy} = S_{xy}/\sqrt{S_{xx}S_{yy}}$. □

In multiple regression, $R^2$ no longer equals a simple function of pairwise correlations, but it always equals the squared correlation between $y_i$ and $\hat{y}_i$. Crucially, adding any predictor to a model — even one that is pure noise — can only leave $R^2$ unchanged or increase it. This makes $R^2$ useless for comparing models with different numbers of predictors. The adjusted $R^2$ corrects for this.

Definition · Adjusted $R^2$

The adjusted coefficient of determination penalizes model complexity: $$\bar{R}^2 = 1 - \frac{\text{SSE}/(n-p)}{\text{SST}/(n-1)} = 1 - (1 - R^2)\frac{n-1}{n-p},$$ where $p$ is the total number of parameters (including the intercept). $\bar{R}^2$ increases when a new predictor is added only if it reduces $\text{MSE} = \text{SSE}/(n-p)$, i.e., only if it contributes more predictive power than would be expected by chance. $\bar{R}^2$ can be negative (when the model fits worse than the intercept-only model after penalization).

Figure 6.5. Three datasets with OLS fits and $R^2$ values of approximately 0.10, 0.82, and 0.99, generated from the same linear model with decreasing noise $\sigma$. Left: High noise — $R^2 \approx 0.10$; the line barely tracks the data but the slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ is meaningful (just imprecisely estimated). Center: Moderate noise — $R^2 \approx 0.82$; a clear linear trend. Right: Low noise — $R^2 \approx 0.99$; near-perfect linear relationship. Crucially, the slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ is interpretable in all three panels. Low $R^2$ means high irreducible noise $\sigma^2$, not that the regression is wrong or useless.

6.7.2 Limitations and Common Pitfalls

Warning · Four Ways $R^2$ Misleads

(1) Spurious regression in time series: Two unrelated trending series (GDP and the number of Nicolas Cage films per year) can yield $R^2 > 0.95$ simply because both trend upward. The high $R^2$ reflects shared nonstationarity, not causal connection. Always first-difference or detrend time series before regressing. (2) Anscombe's Quartet: Four datasets with identical $\bar{x}$, $\bar{y}$, $S_{xx}$, $S_{xy}$, $S_{yy}$, $\hat{\beta}_0$, $\hat{\beta}_1$, $s^2$, and $R^2 = 0.67$ but wildly different scatter plots — one linear, one curved, one with an outlier, one with a perfect line plus a single influential point. $R^2$ cannot distinguish among them. (3) Omitted variables: Adding irrelevant predictors always inflates $R^2$; omitting relevant ones deflates it. A model with $R^2 = 0.15$ may capture the only actionable causal effect; a model with $R^2 = 0.95$ may be spurious. (4) Prediction vs. explanation: $R^2 = 0.95$ in a training set says nothing about out-of-sample prediction if the model is overfit.

Example · High $R^2$ with Wrong Model

Suppose the true model is $y = x^2 + \varepsilon$ with $x \in [-3, 3]$. A linear regression of $y$ on $x$ yields $\hat{\beta}_1 \approx 0$ (the symmetric quadratic has no linear trend), $\hat{\beta}_0 \approx \bar{y} = \mathbb{E}[x^2] = 3$, and $R^2 \approx 0$. But the data clearly follow a deterministic pattern — the issue is that linear regression is the wrong model, not that there is no relationship. Conversely, regressing $y$ on $x^2$ alone gives $R^2 \approx 1$. $R^2$ measures how well a linear model fits, not whether a strong relationship exists.

💡
Key insight: $R^2$ answers only one question: "What fraction of the training-set variance in $Y$ is explained by the linear model?" It does not answer: "Is the model correctly specified?" "Are the coefficients meaningful?" "Will the model predict well on new data?" "Is there a causal relationship?" Use $R^2$ as one of many diagnostics, never as the sole criterion for model quality.
Part III

Multiple Linear Regression

6.8 Matrix Formulation

The simple regression results of Part II extend gracefully to $p - 1$ predictors using matrix notation. The formulas look identical — $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ — but now $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ is a $p$-vector and $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ is a $p \times p$ matrix. The geometric picture from Section 6.2 carries over unchanged: $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is the projection of $\mathbf{y}$ onto the $p$-dimensional column space of $\mathbf{X}$, and the residual is orthogonal to this space.

The central new concept in multiple regression is the partial regression coefficient: $\hat{\beta}_j$ measures the effect of $X_j$ on $Y$ after adjusting for all other predictors. This is both the power and the subtlety of multiple regression — it can disentangle the contributions of correlated predictors, but the coefficients depend heavily on what else is in the model.

6.8.1 Design Matrix and Model

Definition · Design Matrix and Multiple Regression Model

Given $n$ observations and $p-1$ predictors, the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ is the $n \times p$ matrix: $$\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1,p-1} \\ 1 & x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2,p-1} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{n,p-1} \end{pmatrix},$$ with the first column being $\mathbf{1}_n$ (intercept). The multiple linear regression model is: $$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\, \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}_n),$$ where $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\beta_0, \beta_1, \ldots, \beta_{p-1})^\top \in \mathbb{R}^p$ and $\sigma^2 > 0$.

The normal equations $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ from Section 6.2 apply directly, and the solution is unique when $\mathbf{X}$ has full column rank $p$.

Multivariate OLS Estimator
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}, \qquad \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}, \qquad \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$$

6.8.2 Partitioned Regression and the Frisch–Waugh Theorem

One of the most illuminating results in regression theory is the Frisch–Waugh–Lovell (FWL) theorem, which shows that each partial regression coefficient $\hat{\beta}_j$ has a simple two-variable interpretation: it is the slope from regressing the residuals of $Y$ on $\mathbf{X}_{-j}$ against the residuals of $X_j$ on $\mathbf{X}_{-j}$. In other words, each coefficient measures the association between $X_j$ and $Y$ after both have been "partialled out" for all other predictors.

Theorem · Frisch–Waugh–Lovell

Partition $\mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{X}_1 \mid \mathbf{X}_2]$ and let $\mathbf{M}_1 = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{X}_1(\mathbf{X}_1^\top\mathbf{X}_1)^{-1}\mathbf{X}_1^\top$ be the annihilator of $\mathbf{X}_1$. Then the OLS estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_2$ from the full regression of $\mathbf{y}$ on $[\mathbf{X}_1, \mathbf{X}_2]$ equals the OLS estimate from regressing $\mathbf{M}_1\mathbf{y}$ on $\mathbf{M}_1\mathbf{X}_2$. The residuals from both regressions are identical.

Example · Interpreting Partial Coefficients

Setup: Regress salary $Y$ (thousands \$) on experience $X_1$ (years) and education $X_2$ (years), using $n = 200$ employees. The fitted model is $\hat{Y} = 15 + 3.2X_1 + 4.8X_2$.

What $\hat{\beta}_1 = 3.2$ means: Among employees with the same level of education, each additional year of experience is associated with \$3,200 higher predicted salary. This is a partial effect — it controls for education. If experience and education are positively correlated (more experienced employees tend to be more educated), the simple regression slope of salary on experience alone would be larger than 3.2, because it would conflate the experience effect with the education effect.

FWL interpretation: $\hat{\beta}_1 = 3.2$ is the slope from regressing (salary residualized on education) against (experience residualized on education). The residualization removes the shared variance between experience and education, isolating the experience effect.

Omitted variable bias: If we omit $X_2$ from the model and regress only on $X_1$, the slope becomes $\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{simple}} = \hat{\beta}_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 \cdot \hat{\gamma}$, where $\hat{\gamma}$ is the regression coefficient of $X_2$ on $X_1$. If $\hat{\beta}_2 > 0$ and $\hat{\gamma} > 0$ (more experienced $\Rightarrow$ more educated $\Rightarrow$ higher salary), then $\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{simple}} > 3.2$ — positive omitted variable bias.

Data Science Application · Design Matrix in Machine Learning

In machine learning, the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$ is called the feature matrix, and the columns are features. Feature engineering — creating new columns from raw inputs — is exactly the basis expansion idea of Section 6.18. Polynomial features, interaction terms, and log transforms are all just different choices of columns in $\mathbf{X}$. Linear regression with an engineered feature matrix can approximate many nonlinear relationships while retaining the OLS solution and all associated inference machinery. Neural networks can be seen as learning the feature engineering step jointly with the linear regression step.

6.9 Properties of Estimators

The multivariate OLS estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ inherits all the properties of the scalar case: unbiasedness, minimum variance among linear unbiased estimators, and normality (under normal errors). The matrix formulation packages these results compactly and reveals how the covariance structure of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ depends on the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$.

Theorem · Mean and Covariance of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$

Under the linear model with $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}_n$: $$\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}] = \boldsymbol{\beta}, \qquad \text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}.$$ Under normality: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sim \mathcal{N}_p\!\bigl(\boldsymbol{\beta},\, \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\bigr)$, exactly.

Proof

Mean: $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}] = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}] = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta}$. Covariance: Since $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - \boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$: $$\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\underbrace{\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})}_{\sigma^2\mathbf{I}}\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}.$$ Normality: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is a linear transformation of the Gaussian vector $\mathbf{y}$, hence multivariate Gaussian. □

Intuition · What $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ Contains

The $(j,j)$-th diagonal entry of $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ is proportional to $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \sigma^2[(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}]_{jj}$. One can show that $[(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}]_{jj} = 1/(S_{x_jx_j}(1 - R_j^2))$, where $R_j^2$ is the $R^2$ from regressing $X_j$ on all other predictors. When $X_j$ is nearly a linear combination of other predictors ($R_j^2 \to 1$), this entry blows up — the standard error of $\hat{\beta}_j$ becomes enormous, and the coefficient is estimated imprecisely. This is precisely multicollinearity (Section 6.13), encoded directly in the covariance matrix of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$.

Theorem · Multivariate Gauss–Markov

Under (GM1)–(GM3), $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ is BLUE: for any other linear unbiased estimator $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$, the matrix $\text{Cov}(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) - \text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) \succeq \mathbf{0}$ (positive semidefinite). Equivalently, for any fixed vector $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^p$, $\text{Var}(\mathbf{c}^\top\tilde{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) \geq \text{Var}(\mathbf{c}^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})$.

Proof

For any linear unbiased $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{y}$ (where $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}_p$ for unbiasedness), write $\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top + \mathbf{D}$ where $\mathbf{D}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}$. Then: $$\text{Cov}(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\beta}}) = \sigma^2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}^\top = \sigma^2\left[(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}^\top\right] \succeq \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1},$$ since $\sigma^2\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}^\top \succeq \mathbf{0}$ and the cross terms vanish: $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{D}^\top = [(\mathbf{D}\mathbf{X})(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}]^\top = \mathbf{0}$. □

6.10 Inference in Multiple Regression

With $p-1$ predictors rather than one, the inferential toolkit expands significantly. We must now distinguish between testing individual coefficients ($t$-tests), testing all slopes simultaneously (global $F$-test), and testing specific subsets of predictors (partial $F$-tests). These three procedures answer different scientific questions, and confusing them — in particular, using collections of individual $t$-tests to answer a question that should be answered by an $F$-test — is one of the most common errors in applied regression.

The matrix framework of Section 6.8 provides all the distributional machinery. Under $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})$, we have $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} \sim \mathcal{N}_p(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1})$ exactly, and $(n-p)s^2/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-p}$ independently of $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$. These two facts, combined with the definition of the $t$ and $F$ distributions, generate all inference procedures.

6.10.1 Individual $t$-Tests for Each Coefficient

Each coefficient $\hat{\beta}_j$ has a $t$-distribution centered at $\beta_j$, obtained by dividing the normal pivot by $s$ — the same logic as in simple regression. The key difference is that in multiple regression, $\hat{\beta}_j$ is a partial regression coefficient — it measures the effect of $X_j$ holding all other predictors fixed — and its standard error depends on the full inverse $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$, which reflects relationships among all predictors.

Definition · $t$-Statistic and CI for $\beta_j$ in Multiple Regression

Let $v_j = [(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}]_{jj}$ be the $j$-th diagonal entry of the inverse information matrix. The standard error of $\hat{\beta}_j$ is $\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_j) = s\sqrt{v_j}$, where $s^2 = \text{SSE}/(n-p)$. Under $H_0:\beta_j = \beta_j^0$: $$t_j = \frac{\hat{\beta}_j - \beta_j^0}{s\sqrt{v_j}} \sim t_{n-p}.$$ A $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\beta_j$: $$\hat{\beta}_j \pm t_{n-p,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{v_j}.$$ Note: $df = n-p$, not $n-2$, because $p$ parameters (including intercept) are estimated.

Example · Full Output Table — Wage Regression

Model: $\log(\text{Wage}_i) = \beta_0 + \beta_1\,\text{Exp}_i + \beta_2\,\text{Educ}_i + \beta_3\,\text{Female}_i + \varepsilon_i$, with $n = 526$ observations, $p = 4$, $s = 0.441$.

Predictor$\hat{\beta}_j$SE$t$$p$-value95% CI
Intercept0.5140.1034.99<0.0001(0.312, 0.716)
Experience (years)0.01310.00304.37<0.0001(0.0072, 0.0190)
Education (years)0.08530.007112.01<0.0001(0.0714, 0.0993)
Female ($= 1$ if female)$-0.344$0.040$-8.60$<0.0001($-0.423$, $-0.265$)

Interpretation of each row:

  • Experience: Holding education and gender constant, each additional year of experience is associated with a $e^{0.0131} - 1 \approx 1.3\%$ wage premium. The 95% CI is (0.72%, 1.91%). Significant at any conventional level.
  • Education: Each year of schooling is associated with an $\approx 8.9\%$ wage premium, holding experience and gender constant. This is the largest effect and the most precisely estimated.
  • Female: Holding experience and education constant, female workers earn $e^{-0.344} - 1 \approx -29\%$ less than male workers. The CI ($-34\%$, $-23\%$) is entirely negative — a strong, precisely estimated gender wage gap in this sample.
Warning · $t$-Test Results Depend on What Else Is in the Model

The $t$-statistic and $p$-value for $\hat{\beta}_j$ depend on which other predictors are in the model. Adding or removing predictors changes $\hat{\beta}_j$ (due to confounding), $\text{SE}(\hat{\beta}_j)$ (due to multicollinearity), and $s^2$ (due to the changed fit). A predictor that appears significant in simple regression may become non-significant in multiple regression (its effect was due to confounding), or vice versa (it was suppressed by a correlated predictor). Never interpret a $t$-test as though the other predictors are absent — it is always a conditional test: "Is $X_j$ useful given the other predictors already in the model?"

6.10.2 Global $F$-Test: All Slopes Jointly

The global $F$-test asks: "Is there any linear relationship between $\mathbf{y}$ and any of the $p-1$ predictors?" This is the omnibus null hypothesis $H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_{p-1} = 0$. Under $H_0$, knowing $\mathbf{X}$ gives no predictive advantage over just using $\bar{y}$ for every observation.

The test is constructed by comparing the fit of the full model (which uses all $p$ parameters) to the intercept-only model (which uses only 1 parameter). If the full model explains much more variance than it "should" by chance, we reject $H_0$.

Global $F$-Test
