$$F = \frac{\text{SSR}/(p-1)}{\text{SSE}/(n-p)} = \frac{\text{MSR}}{\text{MSE}} \sim F_{p-1,\,n-p} \text{ under } H_0:\beta_1=\cdots=\beta_{p-1}=0$$
Example · Global $F$-Test in the Wage Regression

From the wage regression ($n=526$, $p=4$): $\text{SST} = 108.94$, $\text{SSR} = 108.94 - 101.43 = 57.51$, $\text{SSE} = 101.43 - 57.51= 43.93$...

Actually with $s=0.441$, $s^2=0.1945$, $\text{SSE} = 0.1945\times(526-4) = 101.5$, $\text{SST} = \text{SSE}/(1-R^2)$. Let $R^2 = 0.344$ so $\text{SST} = 154.7$, $\text{SSR} = 0.344\times154.7 = 53.2$.

SourceSSdfMS$F$$p$
Regression (3 predictors)53.22317.7491.2<0.0001
Residual101.55220.1945—
Total154.7525——

$F = 91.2 \gg F_{3,522,0.05} \approx 2.62$. The three predictors collectively explain a highly significant share of wage variation. This global test must be performed before interpreting any individual $t$-test.

6.10.3 Partial $F$-Test: Subsets of Coefficients

The partial $F$-test answers: "After already including some predictors, do additional predictors $X_{q+1}, \ldots, X_{p-1}$ significantly improve the fit?" This tests $H_0: \beta_{q+1} = \cdots = \beta_{p-1} = 0$ in the full model that includes all predictors. It generalizes the individual $t$-test to multiple predictors simultaneously.

The idea is to compare the full model (all predictors) to a reduced model (only the predictors we want to keep). If the full model's residual SSE is much smaller than the reduced model's, the additional predictors are worth including.

Theorem · Partial $F$-Test

Let the reduced model have $p_R$ parameters with SSE$_R$, and the full model have $p_F = p_R + q$ parameters with SSE$_F < $ SSE$_R$. The partial $F$-statistic for $H_0: \boldsymbol{\beta}_\text{extra} = \mathbf{0}$ is: $$F = \frac{(\text{SSE}_R - \text{SSE}_F)/q}{\text{SSE}_F/(n-p_F)} \sim F_{q,\,n-p_F} \text{ under } H_0.$$ Equivalently, $F = [(\text{SSE}_R - \text{SSE}_F)/q]/\text{MSE}_F$. When $q=1$, the partial $F$-statistic equals the square of the individual $t$-statistic for that one coefficient: $F = t_j^2$.

Example · Step-by-Step Partial $F$-Test — House Prices

Question: In a house price regression ($n=200$), does including neighborhood quality ($X_3$) and lot size ($X_4$) significantly improve prediction over a model with just size ($X_1$) and age ($X_2$)?

Reduced model ($X_1, X_2$ only, $p_R=3$): SSE$_R = 42{,}850$.

Full model ($X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$, $p_F=5$): SSE$_F = 31{,}240$.

Partial $F$-statistic ($q = 2$ extra predictors): $$\text{MSE}_F = \frac{31{,}240}{200-5} = 160.2.$$ $$F = \frac{(42{,}850 - 31{,}240)/2}{160.2} = \frac{5{,}805}{160.2} = 36.2.$$

Decision: $F_{2,195,0.05} \approx 3.04$. Since $36.2 \gg 3.04$, strongly reject $H_0$. Neighborhood quality and lot size add highly significant predictive power beyond size and age alone ($p < 0.0001$).

Reporting: "Adding neighborhood quality rating and lot size to the model significantly improved the fit ($F_{2,195} = 36.2$, $p < 0.0001$), reducing residual variance by 27\% (from $42{,}850$ to $31{,}240$)."

Figure 6.10b. Partial $F$-test visualization. Left: Bar chart comparing SSE of the reduced model (only $X_1$, gold) to the full model ($X_1,X_2,X_3$, green). The reduction $\Delta\text{SSE}$ is the variation explained by adding $X_2$ and $X_3$; this is divided by $q=2$ and by MSE$_F$ to form the $F$-statistic. Right: The $F_{2,76}$ distribution under $H_0$; the observed $F_\text{partial}$ (gold dashed) falls far into the rejection region, giving $p \approx 0$, meaning $X_2$ and $X_3$ jointly contribute highly significant predictive power beyond $X_1$ alone.
Intuition · Why Partial $F$ ≠ Two Individual $t$-Tests

If we test $H_0:\beta_3=0$ and $H_0:\beta_4=0$ separately at $\alpha=0.05$, each has 5% type I error probability. The probability that at least one of the two is falsely rejected is up to $1-(0.95)^2 = 9.75\%$ — almost double the intended level. The partial $F$-test for $H_0:\beta_3=\beta_4=0$ jointly controls type I error at exactly 5%. Additionally, when $X_3$ and $X_4$ are correlated (multicollinearity), their individual $t$-tests may be non-significant even though they jointly explain substantial variance — the individual tests fail to capture their combined contribution. The partial $F$-test does.

6.10.4 Confidence Intervals and Prediction Intervals in Multiple Regression

The CI and PI formulas extend naturally to the multivariate setting, with the scalar $(x^*-\bar{x})^2/S_{xx}$ replaced by the multivariate quadratic form $(\mathbf{x}^*)^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}^*$, which measures the distance of $\mathbf{x}^*$ from the data center in the metric of the design.

Definition · CI and PI in Multiple Regression

At new covariate vector $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^p$ (with first entry 1 for the intercept), define $h_{**} = (\mathbf{x}^*)^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}^*$ (the "virtual leverage" of the new point).

CI for $\mathbb{E}[Y|\mathbf{X}=\mathbf{x}^*]$: $$\hat{y}^* \pm t_{n-p,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{h_{**}}.$$ PI for a new observation $Y^*$: $$\hat{y}^* \pm t_{n-p,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{1 + h_{**}}.$$ Both reduce to the simple regression formulas when $p=2$. $h_{**}$ grows when $\mathbf{x}^*$ is far from the observed data joint distribution — detecting "hidden extrapolation" that marginal range checks miss.

Example · CI vs PI — Wage Prediction for a Specific Profile

Model: Wage regression from above. Predict log-wage for a female worker with 8 years experience and 16 years of education.

$\mathbf{x}^* = (1,\; 8,\; 16,\; 1)^\top$. Fitted value: $\hat{y}^* = 0.514 + 0.0131(8) + 0.0853(16) + (-0.344)(1) = 0.514 + 0.105 + 1.365 - 0.344 = 1.640$, corresponding to $e^{1.640} = \$5.15$/hr.

Suppose $h_{**} = 0.015$ (this worker's profile is near the average). Then with $s=0.441$, $t_{522,0.025} \approx 1.965$:

CI: $1.640 \pm 1.965 \times 0.441\sqrt{0.015} = 1.640 \pm 0.106 = (1.534, 1.746)$, i.e., mean wage $\in (e^{1.534}, e^{1.746}) = (\$4.64, \$5.73)$/hr.

PI: $1.640 \pm 1.965 \times 0.441\sqrt{1.015} = 1.640 \pm 0.872 = (0.768, 2.512)$, i.e., individual wage $\in (e^{0.768}, e^{2.512}) = (\$2.16, \$12.33)$/hr.

The PI is 8× wider than the CI, reflecting that individual wages vary enormously even after controlling for experience, education, and gender.

6.10.5 Joint Inference: Confidence Regions for Multiple Coefficients

When interest lies in more than one coefficient simultaneously — e.g., "are both $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ plausible at certain values jointly?" — the correct tool is a joint confidence region, not two separate CIs. The joint region is an ellipsoid in $\mathbb{R}^q$ rather than an interval in $\mathbb{R}^1$.

Definition · Joint $100(1-\alpha)\%$ Confidence Region

For a subvector $\boldsymbol{\beta}_S$ of $q$ coefficients (with OLS estimate $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_S$ and submatrix $\mathbf{V}_S = [(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}]_{S,S}$), the joint confidence region is the ellipsoid: $$\mathcal{E} = \left\{\boldsymbol{\beta}_S : (\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_S - \boldsymbol{\beta}_S)^\top(s^2\mathbf{V}_S)^{-1}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_S - \boldsymbol{\beta}_S) \leq q\,F_{q,n-p,\alpha}\right\}.$$ This region has exact $100(1-\alpha)\%$ coverage under normality. The corresponding "rectangular" region (Cartesian product of individual CIs) is conservative — it always contains the ellipse but may be substantially wider, declaring some $\boldsymbol{\beta}_S$ values impossible that are actually plausible.

Figure 6.10a. Left: Joint 95% confidence region (blue ellipse) for $(\beta_1, \beta_2)$ in a 2-predictor regression, compared to the "rectangular" region formed by the two marginal 95% CIs (gold dashed rectangle). The ellipse is tilted because $\hat{\beta}_1$ and $\hat{\beta}_2$ are correlated (since $X_1$ and $X_2$ are correlated). The rectangle is wider and includes $(\beta_1, \beta_2)$ combinations that the joint test rejects. The green star marks the true $(\beta_1, \beta_2)$, inside the ellipse. Right: The $F_{2,57}$ distribution for the global test $H_0:\beta_1=\beta_2=0$; the observed $F$ (gold) falls deep in the rejection region.
Intuition · Ellipse vs Rectangle — Why the Shape Matters

When $X_1$ and $X_2$ are positively correlated, a large $\hat{\beta}_1$ tends to come with a small $\hat{\beta}_2$ (and vice versa). The joint ellipse captures this trade-off: it is elongated along the direction where the data most easily trade off one coefficient for the other, and narrow in the perpendicular direction where the data are most informative. The rectangle ignores this correlation and is too conservative — it says some $(\beta_1, \beta_2)$ combinations are plausible when the joint ellipse correctly excludes them. In practice, the global $F$-test is the formal version of asking "is the origin $(0,0)$ inside the ellipse?", which is exactly what it tests.

Figure 6.10c. Left: Coefficient plot for a 4-predictor regression ($n=100$). Horizontal lines show 95% CIs for each $\hat{\beta}_j$; diamond markers show the true parameter values. Navy intervals (CIs entirely away from zero) correspond to significant predictors; gray intervals crossing zero are non-significant. Right: $|t|$-statistics for each predictor versus the $\alpha=0.05$ critical value (dashed red line, $t_{95,0.025}\approx1.99$) and $\alpha=0.01$ threshold (dotted gold). $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ exceed the 0.05 threshold; $\beta_3$ and $\beta_4$ do not — their true values are 0.3 and 0.05, too small to detect reliably with $n=100$.
Data Science Application · Inference in High-Dimensional Settings

Classical regression inference breaks down when $p$ approaches or exceeds $n$. With $p \geq n$, OLS does not have a unique solution, $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ does not exist, and all $p$-values from individual $t$-tests are meaningless. Three modern approaches restore valid inference: (1) Lasso + debiasing: Fit lasso to select variables, then refit OLS on the selected set with a bias correction and valid CIs (the "debiased lasso"). (2) Selective inference: Condition on the model selected by lasso or stepwise, adjusting $p$-values for the selection event. (3) Ridge regression with bootstrap SEs: Use parametric or residual bootstrap to estimate the sampling distribution of ridge coefficients. All three are active research areas; classical regression inference is valid only when $p \ll n$ (ideally $p \leq n/10$).

6.11 Prediction in Multiple Regression

Prediction in multiple regression extends the CI and PI framework of Section 6.5 to the multivariate setting, with the scalar expressions replaced by quadratic forms in $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$.

Definition · CI for Mean Response and PI for New Observation (Multiple)

At a new covariate vector $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^p$ (including a leading 1 for the intercept), the fitted value is $\hat{y}^* = (\mathbf{x}^*)^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$. Confidence interval for $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{x}^*]$: $$\hat{y}^* \pm t_{n-p,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{(\mathbf{x}^*)^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}^*}.$$ Prediction interval for a new observation $Y^*$: $$\hat{y}^* \pm t_{n-p,\,\alpha/2} \cdot s\sqrt{1 + (\mathbf{x}^*)^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}^*}.$$

Warning · Hidden Extrapolation in Multiple Dimensions

In simple regression, extrapolation is obvious from a scatter plot: $x^*$ falls outside the range of observed $x_i$. In multiple regression with $p - 1 > 1$ predictors, a prediction point $\mathbf{x}^*$ can fall within the univariate marginal range of each predictor while still lying far outside the joint data cloud. For example, in a regression with height and weight, predicting for a person 7 feet tall and 100 lbs combines values that are individually observed but jointly impossible. The quantity $(\mathbf{x}^*)^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}^*$ — equivalently, $h_{**}$, the diagonal entry of $\mathbf{H}$ for the new point — measures the distance from the data center in the appropriate metric. If $h_{**}$ greatly exceeds $p/n$, the prediction is extrapolation and should be flagged.

Data Science Application · When to Use Prediction Intervals

In machine learning, prediction intervals are rarely computed because models are often nonlinear and lack closed-form variance expressions. But they matter enormously in practice: a medical model predicting length-of-stay that outputs only point predictions without uncertainty bounds gives clinicians no way to assess risk. Linear regression's exact PIs are a significant practical advantage over black-box alternatives. For non-Gaussian regression, conformal prediction provides distribution-free PIs with exact finite-sample coverage guarantees — a modern complement to the classical theory here.

Part IV

Model Diagnostics and Robustness

6.12 Assumption Violations

The four classical assumptions of linear regression — (L) linearity, (I) independence of errors, (N) normality of errors, (E) equal error variance — are often summarized by the acronym LINE. Violations of each have different consequences for bias, efficiency, and validity of inference, and each requires different diagnostics and remedies. Understanding this hierarchy prevents over-reaction to minor violations and under-reaction to serious ones.

Definition · The LINE Assumptions

(L) Linearity: $\mathbb{E}[\mathbf{y}] = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}$. The conditional mean is correctly specified as a linear function of the predictors. This is the most critical assumption — its violation biases all coefficient estimates. (I) Independence: $\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$. Errors are uncorrelated. Violation (autocorrelation, clustering) invalidates standard errors but not point estimates. (N) Normality: $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. Required for exact finite-sample inference; by CLT, large-$n$ inference is approximately valid without it. (E) Equal variance (Homoscedasticity): $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$ for all $i$. Violation makes OLS inefficient (not BLUE) and invalidates standard errors, but not unbiasedness.

AssumptionViolation EffectDetectionRemedy
(L) LinearityBiased $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$; invalid inferenceResid vs. fitted: pattern; component-plus-residual plotsTransformations (§6.16); polynomial/splines (§6.18)
(I) IndependenceSE biased (often too small); OLS not BLUEDurbin-Watson; ACF/PACF of residualsGLS (§6.17); time-series models; cluster-robust SE
(N) NormalityExact $t/F$ invalid; approx. valid for large $n$Q–Q plot; Shapiro-Wilk testBootstrap CIs; robust inference; log transform
(E) HomoscedasticitySE biased; OLS not BLUE; PI coverage offScale-location plot; Breusch-Pagan testWLS (§6.17); HC/HC3 robust SE; log transform
Example · Breusch-Pagan Test for Heteroscedasticity

Setup: After fitting OLS, test whether $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i)$ depends on the predictors.

Procedure: (1) Fit OLS, obtain residuals $e_i$. (2) Regress $e_i^2$ on the predictors $\mathbf{x}_i$. (3) Compute $LM = n \cdot R^2_{\text{aux}}$ from this auxiliary regression. Under $H_0$ (homoscedasticity), $LM \overset{d}{\to} \chi^2_{p-1}$.

Interpretation: A significant LM statistic indicates that residual variance is a function of the predictors — the most common form of heteroscedasticity. The scale-location plot identifies the direction; the BP test provides a formal significance threshold.

Intuition · Hierarchy of Assumption Severity

Linearity is the most critical because it biases the estimates themselves — you get wrong answers. Independence violations bias standard errors without biasing estimates — you get right answers with wrong uncertainty quantification. Heteroscedasticity has the same effect as independence violations. Normality is the least critical: by the CLT, $t$-statistics are approximately standard normal for $n \gtrsim 30$ regardless of the error distribution, and OLS point estimates are always unbiased and consistent. The practical ordering of concern: linearity $\gg$ independence $\approx$ homoscedasticity $\gg$ normality.

6.13 Multicollinearity

Multicollinearity refers to near-linear dependence among the columns of the design matrix $\mathbf{X}$. It does not cause bias — OLS remains unbiased — but it inflates the variances of individual coefficient estimates, making them imprecise, numerically unstable, and sensitive to small changes in the data. Understanding the diagnosis and consequences of multicollinearity is essential before interpreting any multiple regression output.

6.13.1 Detection: VIF and Condition Number

Definition · Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)

For predictor $X_j$ in a multiple regression, the variance inflation factor is: $$\text{VIF}_j = \frac{1}{1 - R_j^2},$$ where $R_j^2$ is the coefficient of determination from regressing $X_j$ on all other predictors. The variance of $\hat{\beta}_j$ satisfies: $$\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_j) = \frac{\sigma^2 \cdot \text{VIF}_j}{S_{x_jx_j}}.$$ Conventional thresholds: VIF $> 5$ warrants attention; VIF $> 10$ indicates severe multicollinearity; VIF $= 1$ means $X_j$ is orthogonal to all other predictors (ideal).

Definition · Condition Number

The condition number of $\mathbf{X}$ is $\kappa(\mathbf{X}) = \sigma_{\max}(\mathbf{X})/\sigma_{\min}(\mathbf{X})$, the ratio of largest to smallest singular values. Large condition numbers indicate near-collinearity. Conventional thresholds: $\kappa > 30$ is moderate; $\kappa > 100$ is severe. Since $\kappa(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}) = \kappa(\mathbf{X})^2$, forming the normal equations squares the numerical difficulty.

Figure 6.8. Multicollinearity effects in a two-predictor regression ($n=100$). Left: The $(1,1)$ entry of $(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ (proportional to $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1)$) as a function of $r(x_1, x_2)$. Variance stays nearly constant for $|r| < 0.7$ but explodes as $|r| \to 1$. Right: VIF $= 1/(1-r^2)$ for the same range. Blue bars (VIF $< 5$) are acceptable; gold ($5\leq\text{VIF}<10$) moderate; red (VIF $\geq 10$) severe. At $r=0.99$, VIF $\approx 50$ — standard errors are inflated $\sqrt{50} \approx 7$-fold relative to the orthogonal design.

6.13.2 Consequences and Remedies

Warning · Symptoms of Severe Multicollinearity

The classic warning signs: (1) A highly significant global $F$-test ($p < 0.001$) but no individually significant $t$-tests ($p > 0.1$ for all slopes) — the predictors collectively explain a lot, but individually each can be replaced by the others. (2) Coefficient estimates that flip sign or change dramatically when one predictor is added or removed. (3) Very large standard errors relative to the point estimates, leading to wide confidence intervals that span zero even for clearly important variables. (4) Predictions remain reliable for covariate vectors in the observed data joint distribution, but extrapolation is very dangerous. Multicollinearity afflicts the coefficients, not the predictions.

Data Science Application · Remedies for Multicollinearity

Four principal remedies: (1) Remove predictors: If two predictors are highly correlated ($r > 0.95$), dropping one rarely hurts predictions significantly and greatly stabilizes the remaining coefficient. (2) Principal Component Regression (PCR): Replace $\mathbf{X}$ with its leading $k$ principal components (which are orthogonal by construction), then regress $\mathbf{y}$ on these components. Coefficients lose direct interpretability but stability is restored. (3) Ridge regression: Minimize $\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 + \lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2$ to get $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} + \lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$. The added diagonal $\lambda\mathbf{I}$ makes $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} + \lambda\mathbf{I}$ invertible regardless of multicollinearity. (4) Collect more data: With sufficiently large $n$, the signal can be distinguished even among correlated predictors.

6.14 Influential and High-Leverage Points

Individual observations can affect the fitted regression in two qualitatively different ways. A point with an unusual $y$-value (large residual, given its $x$) is an outlier in the response direction. A point with unusual $x$-values (far from the rest of the data in predictor space) has high leverage. An observation that actually changes the fitted model substantially — shifting the estimated coefficients — is influential. Cook's distance combines outlier-ness and leverage into a single influence measure.

Definition · Leverage, Cook's Distance, and DFBETAS

The leverage of observation $i$ is $h_{ii} = [\mathbf{H}]_{ii} = \mathbf{x}_i^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i$, satisfying $1/n \leq h_{ii} \leq 1$ and $\sum_i h_{ii} = p$. Convention: flag $h_{ii} > 2p/n$. Cook's distance measures the shift in all fitted values when observation $i$ is deleted: $$D_i = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^n(\hat{y}_j - \hat{y}_{j(i)})^2}{p\,s^2} = \frac{r_i^2}{p} \cdot \frac{h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}},$$ where $\hat{y}_{j(i)}$ is the fitted value for observation $j$ when $i$ is removed from the fit, and $r_i$ is the standardized residual. Convention: flag $D_i > 4/n$ (liberal) or $D_i > 1$ (conservative). DFBETAS$_{ij} = (\hat{\beta}_j - \hat{\beta}_{j(i)}) / (s_{(i)}\sqrt{[(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}]_{jj}})$ measures the standardized change in $\hat{\beta}_j$ when observation $i$ is deleted. Flag $|\text{DFBETAS}_{ij}| > 2/\sqrt{n}$.

Figure 6.7. Left: $n=40$ observations with two high-leverage points (red triangles) at extreme $x$-values ($x=0.05$ and $x=9.95$), far from the bulk of the data near $[1,8]$. The OLS line is shown; these high-leverage points happen to fall near it, so their Cook's distances are moderate. Right: Cook's distances $D_i$ for all observations; red dots exceed the $4/n$ threshold (dashed line). The two extreme points have the largest $D_i$ values. If their $y$-values were anomalous, their influence would be severe — this is the key: leverage measures potential influence, not realized influence.
Example · Distinguishing Three Types of Problematic Points

Type A — Response outlier, low leverage: A point near $x = \bar{x}$ (leverage $h_{ii} \approx p/n$) but with $|r_i| = 4$ (4 SD from the fitted line). It inflates $s^2$ substantially and is detected by the Q–Q plot and residuals vs. fitted plot. Cook's $D_i = r_i^2 h_{ii}/[p(1-h_{ii})] \approx (16 \cdot p/n) / [p(1-p/n)]$ — moderate. It pulls the slope somewhat but not dramatically.

Type B — High leverage, small residual: A point at an extreme $x$-value ($h_{ii} = 0.4$) with $|r_i| = 0.2$. It has high leverage but a small residual — it falls near the regression line and is actually reinforcing the fit. $D_i \approx 0.04 \cdot 0.4/(1 \cdot 0.6)$ — negligible. Not influential despite high leverage.

Type C — High leverage, large residual (the dangerous case): A point with $h_{ii} = 0.4$ and $|r_i| = 3$. $D_i \approx 9 \cdot 0.4/(1 \cdot 0.6) = 6$ — large. This observation is both extreme in $x$ and discrepant in $y$; it substantially changes the fit when removed. This is the combination that warrants careful investigation and potentially robust regression (Section 6.15).

Warning · Never Delete Outliers Without Investigation

An influential observation with a large residual is not automatically an error. It might represent a genuine extreme event — a market crash, a biological maximum, an exceptional individual — and discarding it throws away real information. The correct protocol: (1) Check whether the data point is a transcription or measurement error; if so, correct or delete it. (2) If the point is genuine, report the analysis both with and without it. (3) If conclusions change materially, the analysis is fragile and robust methods (M-estimators, Section 6.15) should be the primary analysis. Deleting influential points to improve $R^2$ or $p$-values is a form of p-hacking.

6.15 Robust Regression

Ordinary least squares minimizes $\sum e_i^2$. The quadratic penalty assigns rapidly increasing weight to large residuals — a single observation with $|e_i| = 10\sigma$ contributes $100\sigma^2$ to the criterion, dwarfing the contributions of all well-behaved observations. This sensitivity to outliers is OLS's primary weakness in adversarial or heavy-tailed settings. Robust regression methods replace the squared loss with a loss function that grows more slowly for large residuals, limiting the influence of outliers.

6.15.1 M-Estimators

Definition · M-Estimators and Huber Loss

An M-estimator minimizes $\sum_{i=1}^n \rho(e_i / s)$ over $\boldsymbol{\beta}$, where $\rho$ is a loss function and $s$ is a robust scale estimate such as $s = \text{MAD}(e_i)/0.6745$ (the median absolute deviation, scaled to be consistent for $\sigma$ under normality). The Huber loss with tuning constant $k = 1.345$ is: $$\rho_k(u) = \begin{cases} u^2/2 & |u| \leq k \\ k|u| - k^2/2 & |u| > k. \end{cases}$$ The corresponding influence function is $\psi_k(u) = \rho_k'(u) = \min(k, \max(-k, u))$ — linear within $[-k, k]$ (like OLS) and bounded outside (unlike OLS). With $k = 1.345$, the Huber estimator achieves 95% asymptotic efficiency relative to OLS under normality while providing substantial robustness to outliers.

Definition · Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS)

M-estimators are computed via IRLS: starting from the OLS estimate, iterate:

  1. Compute residuals $e_i^{(t)} = y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{(t)}$ and scale $s^{(t)} = \text{MAD}(e_i^{(t)})/0.6745$.
  2. Compute weights $w_i^{(t)} = \psi_k(e_i^{(t)}/s^{(t)}) / (e_i^{(t)}/s^{(t)})$.
  3. Update: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}^{(t+1)} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{W}^{(t)}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{W}^{(t)}\mathbf{y}$, where $\mathbf{W}^{(t)} = \text{diag}(w_i^{(t)})$.
Each iteration solves a weighted least squares problem. Observations with large residuals receive smaller weights in subsequent iterations, down-weighting their influence.

6.15.2 RANSAC and Breakdown Point

Definition · Breakdown Point

The breakdown point of an estimator is the largest fraction of contaminated observations that can be tolerated before the estimate can become arbitrarily biased. OLS has breakdown point $1/n \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$ — a single outlier can make $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ arbitrarily bad. The Least Median of Squares (LMS) estimator has breakdown point $50\%$ — it gives correct estimates even when nearly half the data is contaminated.

Figure 6.14. $n=48$ observations from $y=1+1.5x+\varepsilon$ ($\varepsilon\overset{\text{i.i.d.}}{\sim}\mathcal{N}(0,0.64)$), plus 3 outliers (red X marks) at $x\in\{8.8,9.0,9.2\}$ with $y\approx -8$ (severely low). OLS (dashed red line, $\hat{\beta}_1\approx0.4$) is strongly pulled toward the outliers, substantially underestimating the true slope of 1.5. The Huber M-estimator (green line, $\hat{\beta}_1\approx1.45$) correctly recovers the true relationship by assigning near-zero weight to the three discrepant observations after just a few IRLS iterations.
Intuition · RANSAC for High Breakdown Point

RANSAC (Random Sample Consensus) is an algorithmic approach to robust regression used widely in computer vision. It iterates: (1) randomly sample the minimum number of observations needed to fit the model ($p$ observations for linear regression); (2) fit the model on this subsample; (3) count "inliers" — observations whose residual falls within a tolerance $\tau$; (4) keep the fit with the most inliers. After many iterations, the best fit corresponds to the largest consistent subset of the data. The remaining points (large residuals on the best-fitting model) are the outliers. RANSAC can tolerate outlier fractions approaching 50% with enough iterations, achieving a breakdown point similar to LMS but with better computational properties.

Part V

Transformations and Extensions

6.16 Transforming to Linearity

The linear regression framework requires that $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]$ is a linear function of $x$ — not that $Y$ itself is linearly related to $X$ in the raw scale. When the raw relationship is nonlinear, transformations of $Y$, $X$, or both can achieve linearity. This is not merely a technical trick: a well-chosen transformation often simultaneously linearizes the relationship, stabilizes the error variance, and symmetrizes the error distribution, addressing multiple assumption violations at once.

6.16.1 Common Transformations

Definition · Box–Cox Power Transformation

For strictly positive response $Y > 0$, the Box–Cox family is: $$y^{(\lambda)} = \begin{cases} (y^\lambda - 1)/\lambda & \lambda \neq 0 \\ \log y & \lambda = 0. \end{cases}$$ The parameter $\lambda$ is chosen to maximize the profile log-likelihood: $$\ell(\lambda) = -\frac{n}{2}\log\!\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i^{(\lambda)} - \hat{y}_i^{(\lambda)})^2\right] + (\lambda - 1)\sum_{i=1}^n\log y_i.$$ A 95% confidence interval for $\lambda$ can be constructed by profiling, and commonly-interpretable values ($\lambda \in \{-1, -0.5, 0, 0.5, 1\}$) should be tested for compatibility with this interval.

$\lambda$TransformationTypical application
$-1$Reciprocal $1/Y$Rate data, reaction times
$-0.5$Reciprocal square rootVariance inverse proportional to mean$^4$
$0$Log $\log Y$Multiplicative errors, counts, income, prices
$0.5$Square root $\sqrt{Y}$Poisson data, variance stabilization
$1$Identity (no transform)Already linear and homoscedastic
$2$Square $Y^2$Response bounded below; left-skewed distribution
Example · Log-Linear Model and Interpretation

Setup: House prices $Y$ (thousands \$) are regressed on living area $X_1$ (sq ft) and lot size $X_2$ (acres). Diagnostic plots on the raw model show a funnel pattern (heteroscedasticity) and curved residuals vs. fitted (nonlinearity). A Box–Cox analysis suggests $\hat{\lambda} \approx 0.05$, compatible with $\lambda = 0$ (log transform). We fit: $\log Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \varepsilon$.

Interpretation of $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.0003$: This is a semi-elasticity: a one sq ft increase in area is associated with a $100 \times (e^{0.0003} - 1) \approx 0.03\%$ increase in the median house price. Equivalently, an extra 100 sq ft multiplies median price by $e^{0.03} \approx 1.030$ (3% premium).

Median vs. mean: Under the log-linear model with normal errors $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$, $\mathbb{E}[Y \mid \mathbf{X}] = e^{\mathbf{x}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta} + \sigma^2/2}$ but $\text{median}[Y \mid \mathbf{X}] = e^{\mathbf{x}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}}$. The back-transform $e^{\hat{\mathbf{x}}^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}}$ predicts the median price (the geometric mean), not the arithmetic mean. For predicting the mean, use $e^{\hat{\mathbf{x}}^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} + s^2/2}$.

Figure 6.10. Data generated from $Y = 2e^{0.7x} \cdot \varepsilon^*$ with multiplicative lognormal noise ($n=80$, $x\in[0.5,5]$, $\sigma_{\log}=0.3$). Top left: Raw scale — OLS line (red) fits poorly; Bottom left: residuals show a severe funnel pattern indicating heteroscedasticity and slight curvature indicating nonlinearity. After log-transforming $Y$ (top right): the OLS line (navy) fits excellently; bottom right residuals are homoscedastic with no pattern. One transformation simultaneously fixed both violations.

6.16.2 Variance-Stabilizing Transformations

Intuition · The Delta Method and Variance Stabilization

If $\text{Var}(Y) \approx g(\mathbb{E}[Y])^2$ for some function $g$ (mean-variance relationship), then the variance-stabilizing transformation is $h(Y) = \int 1/g(\mu)\,d\mu$. By the delta method, $\text{Var}(h(Y)) \approx [h'(\mathbb{E}[Y])]^2 \text{Var}(Y) = [1/g(\mu)]^2 g(\mu)^2 = 1$ — approximately constant. Key examples: Poisson data ($\text{Var}(Y) = \mu$, so $g(\mu) = \sqrt{\mu}$) $\to$ $h(Y) = \sqrt{Y}$. Binomial proportions ($\text{Var}(Y/n) \approx p(1-p)/n$) $\to$ arcsin$(\sqrt{Y/n})$. Lognormal ($\text{Var}(Y) \approx \mu^2\sigma_{\log}^2$) $\to$ $\log Y$.

6.17 Weighted and Generalized Least Squares

When the error covariance structure is known — or can be estimated — we can obtain more efficient estimates than OLS by accounting for the dependence structure. Weighted least squares (WLS) handles heteroscedastic errors; generalized least squares (GLS) handles arbitrary correlation structures. Both are special cases of the GLS framework, which generalizes the Gauss–Markov theorem to the correlated setting (Aitken's theorem).

Definition · Generalized Least Squares (GLS)

Assume $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ with $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ where $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ is a known symmetric positive definite matrix. The GLS estimator is: $$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{GLS}} = (\mathbf{X}^\top\boldsymbol{\Omega}^{-1}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\boldsymbol{\Omega}^{-1}\mathbf{y},$$ with $\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{GLS}}) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\boldsymbol{\Omega}^{-1}\mathbf{X})^{-1}$. Weighted least squares (WLS) is the special case $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = \text{diag}(1/w_1, \ldots, 1/w_n)$ — observations with larger weights $w_i$ contribute more to the fit, as they have smaller error variance $\sigma^2/w_i$.

Theorem · Aitken's Theorem (Generalized Gauss–Markov)

Under $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^2\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ with $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ known and positive definite, the GLS estimator $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{GLS}}$ is BLUE among all linear unbiased estimators of $\boldsymbol{\beta}$.

Proof Sketch

Factor $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = \mathbf{P}^\top\mathbf{P}$ (Cholesky decomposition). Transform: $\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = (\mathbf{P}^\top)^{-1}\mathbf{y}$, $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = (\mathbf{P}^\top)^{-1}\mathbf{X}$, $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}} = (\mathbf{P}^\top)^{-1}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$. Then: $$\text{Cov}(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}) = (\mathbf{P}^\top)^{-1}\sigma^2\boldsymbol{\Omega}(\mathbf{P}^{-1}) = (\mathbf{P}^\top)^{-1}\sigma^2\mathbf{P}^\top\mathbf{P}\,(\mathbf{P}^\top)^{-1} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}.$$ The transformed model $\tilde{\mathbf{y}} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \tilde{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}}$ has i.i.d. errors, so OLS on the transformed problem is BLUE by the standard Gauss–Markov theorem. This OLS equals GLS on the original problem. □

Figure 6.15. $n=60$ observations with heteroscedastic errors: $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = (0.3x_i)^2$ — variance proportional to $x^2$. Left: OLS (dashed red) and WLS with weights $w_i = 1/x_i^2$ (green line). Both yield similar slopes here, but WLS has substantially smaller standard errors (more efficient). Observations are color-coded by weight: darker blue = higher weight = smaller variance. Right: Residual comparison — OLS residuals (red) show the funnel pattern; WLS residuals (green) are homoscedastic, confirming assumption satisfaction.
Example · Feasible GLS When $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ Is Unknown

In practice, $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ is rarely known. Feasible GLS (FGLS) proceeds in two steps: (1) Fit OLS, obtain residuals $e_i$, and model $\log(e_i^2) = \gamma_0 + \gamma_1 x_i$ (or more complex functions) to estimate $\hat{\sigma}_i^2 = e^{\hat{\gamma}_0 + \hat{\gamma}_1 x_i}$. (2) Set $w_i = 1/\hat{\sigma}_i^2$ and fit WLS. FGLS is consistent (converges to the true $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ as $n \to \infty$) and more efficient than OLS when heteroscedasticity is strong, but is not BLUE in finite samples since $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ must be estimated. An alternative that requires no estimation of $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ is to use heteroscedasticity-consistent (HC3) standard errors — these are robust to any form of heteroscedasticity and are the default in modern applied econometrics.

6.18 Polynomial and Basis Expansion

When $\mu(x) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid X = x]$ is genuinely nonlinear in $x$ and no simple parametric transformation achieves linearity, we can approximate $\mu$ by a basis expansion: represent it as a linear combination of $K$ basis functions $\phi_1(x), \ldots, \phi_K(x)$. The model $y_i = \sum_{k=1}^K \beta_k \phi_k(x_i) + \varepsilon_i$ is still linear in the parameters} $\beta_1, \ldots, \beta_K$, so OLS applies directly and all inference machinery (standard errors, $t$-tests, confidence bands) carries over without modification.

6.18.1 Polynomial Regression

Definition · Polynomial Regression

Polynomial regression of degree $d$ uses basis functions $\phi_k(x) = x^{k-1}$ for $k = 1, \ldots, d+1$: $$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \beta_2 x_i^2 + \cdots + \beta_d x_i^d + \varepsilon_i.$$ The design matrix has columns $[1, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}^2, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^d]$ where $\mathbf{x}^k = (x_1^k, \ldots, x_n^k)^\top$. OLS on this design matrix is mathematically identical to multiple regression with $p = d + 1$ parameters.

Polynomial regression can approximate any smooth function arbitrarily well as $d$ increases, by the Weierstrass approximation theorem. However, high-degree polynomials suffer from Runge's phenomenon: wild oscillations near the endpoints of the data range, even when the fit is excellent in the interior. They are also numerically unstable (the Vandermonde matrix is highly ill-conditioned) and lose interpretability — a degree-8 polynomial coefficient has no natural meaning.

6.18.2 Splines

Definition · Natural Cubic Spline

A natural cubic spline with $K$ interior knots $\xi_1 < \cdots < \xi_K$ is a function $f$ that:

  • Is a cubic polynomial on each interval $(-\infty, \xi_1)$, $(\xi_1, \xi_2)$, $\ldots$, $(\xi_K, \infty)$.
  • Has continuous first and second derivatives at each knot $\xi_k$ (smooth joins).
  • Is linear (not cubic) on the tails $x < \xi_1$ and $x > \xi_K$ (natural boundary conditions).
It can be represented using a basis of $K + 2$ functions, and OLS on this basis is BLUE for the linear approximation to $\mu$ within the natural cubic spline space. Compared to polynomial regression, splines avoid Runge's phenomenon, are numerically stable, and are much more locally adaptive.

Intuition · LOESS (Locally Estimated Scatterplot Smoothing)

LOESS fits a local weighted polynomial at each point $x_0$: observations near $x_0$ receive high weight, those far away receive zero weight. The weight function is typically $W(x_i; x_0) = (1 - |x_i - x_0|^3/\Delta^3)^3$ (tricube kernel) where $\Delta$ is the bandwidth controlling the neighborhood size. A large bandwidth gives a smooth, biased fit (like a global low-degree polynomial); a small bandwidth gives a wiggly, low-bias fit (like interpolation). LOESS is ideal for exploratory analysis and diagnostic plots (it underlies the smooth curves in residual plots), but does not produce interpretable coefficients or formal standard errors in the usual sense.

Figure 6.11. $n=60$ observations from $\mu(x)=\sin(x)+0.5\cos(2x)$ with $\sigma=0.4$, $x\in[0,10]$. The true function (green dashed line) has two oscillations. Degree-3 polynomial (gold) captures the general trend but misses the oscillations. Degree-10 polynomial (red) overfits — it follows the training data closely but oscillates wildly near the boundaries (Runge's phenomenon). The smoothing spline (navy line) closely tracks the true function throughout the range while remaining smooth, demonstrating superior bias-variance balance.
Data Science Application · Generalized Additive Models

In a multiple regression setting, polynomial and spline basis expansions can be applied to each predictor independently. A Generalized Additive Model (GAM) replaces the linear term $\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top\mathbf{x}$ with $\sum_{j=1}^{p-1} f_j(x_j)$, where each $f_j$ is estimated nonparametrically (typically as a spline). GAMs retain additivity — a major interpretability advantage — while allowing nonlinear effects. They are fit using backfitting algorithms and are available in R (mgcv package) and Python (pygam). The penalty on spline roughness is analogous to ridge regularization and controls the bias-variance trade-off.

6.19 Categorical and Interaction Effects

Not all predictors are continuous. Group membership, experimental treatment, geographic region, season — these are categorical variables that must be encoded before entering the design matrix. The standard approach is indicator (dummy) coding, which represents a $K$-level factor as $K-1$ binary variables. Interaction terms then allow the effect of one predictor to vary across levels of another.

6.19.1 Indicator Variables and Reference Coding

Definition · Indicator Coding and Reference Category

For a categorical variable with $K$ levels, define $K - 1$ indicator (dummy) variables: $$D_{ik} = \mathbb{1}[\text{observation } i \text{ belongs to group } k], \quad k = 2, \ldots, K.$$ The omitted level ($k = 1$) is the reference category. The model: $$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_2 D_{i2} + \cdots + \beta_K D_{iK} + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$$ gives $\beta_0$ as the intercept for the reference group, and $\beta_k$ as the mean difference between group $k$ and the reference group, holding $x$ fixed. Including all $K$ indicators creates perfect multicollinearity (the "dummy variable trap": the $K$ indicators sum to the intercept column).

Example · Wage Regression with Education Level

Setup: Regress log-wage $Y$ on experience $X_1$ (years) and education level (3 categories: High School = reference, Bachelors = $D_2$, Graduate = $D_3$), with $n = 500$ workers.

Model: $\log\hat{Y} = 2.8 + 0.032 X_1 + 0.41 D_2 + 0.78 D_3$.

Interpretation: $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.032$: each additional year of experience is associated with a $\approx 3.2\%$ wage premium, holding education constant. $\hat{\beta}_2 = 0.41$: Bachelors degree holders earn $e^{0.41} - 1 \approx 51\%$ more than high school graduates with the same experience. $\hat{\beta}_3 = 0.78$: Graduate degree holders earn $e^{0.78} - 1 \approx 118\%$ more than high school graduates.

Testing equality of all education effects: $H_0: \beta_2 = \beta_3 = 0$ is tested by the partial $F$-test comparing this model to one with only $X_1$. With SSE$_F = 420$ and SSE$_R = 615$ ($n = 500$, $p = 4$, $q = 2$): $F = (615 - 420)/2 \div (420/496) = 97.5/0.847 = 115 \gg F_{2,496,0.05} \approx 3.01$. Education effects are overwhelmingly significant.

6.19.2 Interaction Terms and ANCOVA

Definition · Interaction Effects

An interaction term between a continuous predictor $X_1$ and a group indicator $D_2$ is $D_2 \times X_1$, the product. The model: $$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1} + \beta_2 D_{i2} + \beta_{12}(D_{i2} \cdot x_{i1}) + \varepsilon_i$$ implies:

  • Group 1 ($D_{i2} = 0$): $\mathbb{E}[Y_i] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{i1}$ (slope $= \beta_1$, intercept $= \beta_0$)
  • Group 2 ($D_{i2} = 1$): $\mathbb{E}[Y_i] = (\beta_0 + \beta_2) + (\beta_1 + \beta_{12})x_{i1}$ (slope $= \beta_1 + \beta_{12}$, intercept $= \beta_0 + \beta_2$)
When $\beta_{12} = 0$, the groups have parallel regression lines (same slope, different intercepts — the ANCOVA model). When $\beta_{12} \neq 0$, the slopes differ. The test $H_0: \beta_{12} = 0$ is a standard $t$-test on the interaction coefficient.

Figure 6.12. $n=80$ observations in two groups (blue and gold), $x\in[0,10]$. Left: No interaction ($\beta_{12}=0$) — both groups have the same slope; the regression lines are parallel. The group coefficient $\beta_2=3$ is a constant vertical shift. Right: With interaction ($\beta_{12}=1.2$) — group 0 slope $=1.5$, group 1 slope $=1.5+1.2=2.7$. The lines are no longer parallel; the effect of $x$ on $Y$ depends on group membership. The lines cross (if extended), indicating a qualitative interaction at lower $x$ values.
Warning · Interpreting Main Effects in Models with Interactions

When an interaction $X_1 \times X_2$ is present, the "main effect" coefficient on $X_1$ is the partial derivative $\partial\mathbb{E}[Y]/\partial X_1|_{X_2=0}$ — the effect of $X_1$ when $X_2 = 0$. If $X_2 = 0$ is not in the range of the observed data, this is an extrapolation that may be meaningless. Remedy: Center continuous variables before forming interactions: replace $X_2$ with $\tilde{X}_2 = X_2 - \bar{X}_2$ so that the main effect of $X_1$ is evaluated at the mean of $X_2$, which is in the data range and is interpretable. This also reduces multicollinearity between $X_1$, $X_2$, and $X_1 X_2$.

Part VI

Model Selection and Generalization

6.20 Overfitting

A model that fits the training data well but generalizes poorly to new observations is said to be overfit. Overfitting occurs when model complexity — number of parameters, degree of polynomial, density of knots — exceeds what the data can support. An overfit model has learned the noise in the training sample rather than the underlying signal, and its predictions on new data can be far worse than a simpler model's. Understanding overfitting concretely is prerequisite to model selection.

Definition · Training Error, Test Error, and the Generalization Gap

Let $\mathcal{D}_{\text{tr}} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n$ be the training set and $\mathcal{D}_{\text{te}} = \{(x_j^*, y_j^*)\}_{j=1}^{n_{\text{te}}}$ be a held-out test set, both from the same distribution $P_{XY}$. The training MSE and test MSE are: $$\text{MSE}_{\text{tr}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i - \hat{f}(x_i))^2, \qquad \text{MSE}_{\text{te}} = \frac{1}{n_{\text{te}}}\sum_{j=1}^{n_{\text{te}}}(y_j^* - \hat{f}(x_j^*))^2.$$ The generalization gap is $\text{MSE}_{\text{te}} - \text{MSE}_{\text{tr}} \geq 0$. Overfitting is diagnosed when this gap is large. For OLS with $p$ predictors: $\mathbb{E}[\text{MSE}_{\text{tr}}] = \sigma^2(1 - p/n)$ and $\mathbb{E}[\text{MSE}_{\text{te}}] = \sigma^2(1 + p/n)$, giving an expected gap of $2\sigma^2 p/n$ — growing linearly with the number of parameters per observation.

Figure 6.9. $n=20$ training points from $\mu(x)=\sin(x)$ with $\sigma=0.5$, $x\in[-3,3]$. True function shown as green dashed line. Left (degree 1): Underfit — the line misses the curvature; both training and test MSE are similarly poor ($\approx0.48$). Center (degree 4): Near-optimal — captures the main shape; training MSE $\approx0.16$, test MSE $\approx0.20$ (small gap). Right (degree 15): Severe overfit — the polynomial interpolates training points but oscillates wildly; training MSE near zero, test MSE $\gg1$. The model has memorized noise rather than learned signal.
Intuition · Degrees of Freedom and the Gap

A model with $p$ parameters has $p$ "degrees of freedom" available to fit the training data. When $p = n$, a linear model can interpolate every training point exactly — training MSE = 0 — but has learned $n$ specific noise values, not the signal. The generalization gap scales as $p/n$: 100 parameters with 1000 observations gives a modest gap; 100 parameters with 110 observations gives a huge one. This is the operational content of the rule-of-thumb "at least 10–30 observations per predictor." Below this threshold, even a correctly specified model will overfit badly.

6.21 Bias–Variance Trade-off

The bias–variance decomposition from Section 6.1 is not merely a theoretical formula — it is a diagnostic and design tool. Every model choice along the complexity axis (number of predictors, polynomial degree, regularization strength) trades bias for variance or vice versa. The goal of model selection is to find the complexity level that minimizes total expected test MSE, which requires estimating both the bias and variance components from data.

6.21.1 Bias–Variance in Linear Models

Theorem · Bias–Variance for OLS Under Correct Specification

If the true model is $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$ with $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2$, then OLS has: $$\text{Bias}(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}_0)) = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}_0)) = \sigma^2 \mathbf{x}_0^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_0.$$ The expected in-sample MSE is $\mathbb{E}[\text{MSE}_{\text{tr}}] = \sigma^2(n - p)/n$ and expected test MSE (at a new $\mathbf{x}_0$ from the same distribution) is $\sigma^2(n + p)/n$. Both converge to $\sigma^2$ as $n \to \infty$ with fixed $p$, but the gap persists for finite $n$.

6.21.2 Regularization as Bias–Variance Control

Data Science Application · Ridge, Lasso, and the Bias–Variance Trade-off

Ridge regression minimizes $\|\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 + \lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2$, giving $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X} + \lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$. Using the SVD $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{D}\mathbf{V}^\top$, $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda = \sum_{j=1}^p \frac{d_j^2}{d_j^2 + \lambda}\mathbf{v}_j\mathbf{u}_j^\top\mathbf{y}/d_j$: each SVD component is shrunk by $d_j^2/(d_j^2 + \lambda) < 1$. Ridge introduces bias (all components shrunk toward zero) but reduces variance. The optimal $\lambda$ trades the two, minimizing test MSE. Lasso ($L_1$ penalty $\lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1$) achieves sparse solutions — some $\hat{\beta}_j = 0$ exactly — via the geometry of the $L_1$ ball. Lasso simultaneously performs variable selection and regularization, biasing selected coefficients toward zero but setting irrelevant ones exactly to zero. The optimal $\lambda$ for both is found via cross-validation (Section 6.22).

Intuition · Learning Curves

A learning curve plots training and test error versus sample size $n$. For a well-specified model, the pattern is: (1) with small $n$, the model fits the few training points well (low training error) but has high test error (high variance — different small samples give very different fits); (2) as $n$ grows, training error increases (harder to fit more data) while test error decreases (more data reduces variance); (3) both curves converge to the irreducible noise level $\sigma^2$ as $n \to \infty$. If the model is misspecified (too simple), the curves converge but to a level above $\sigma^2$ (residual bias). Learning curves are a powerful diagnostic: a large gap between training and test error at large $n$ indicates overfitting; both curves plateauing above $\sigma^2$ indicates underfitting.

6.22 Model Selection Techniques

Model selection asks: among a collection of candidate models, which produces the best predictions on new data? The answer requires estimating out-of-sample (test) error from the training data alone, without access to a separate test set. Three families of methods accomplish this: information criteria (AIC, BIC) which analytically correct training error for model complexity; cross-validation which empirically estimates test error via repeated train-test splits; and regularization paths which trace the OLS solution as a function of a penalty parameter, implicitly searching over model complexity.

6.22.1 Information Criteria

Definition · AIC and BIC

For a model with $p$ parameters fit by maximum likelihood (under normality, equivalent to OLS): $$\text{AIC} = n\log\!\left(\frac{\text{SSE}}{n}\right) + 2p, \qquad \text{BIC} = n\log\!\left(\frac{\text{SSE}}{n}\right) + p\log n.$$ Lower values of AIC or BIC indicate better models. AIC is asymptotically efficient: it selects the model minimizing test MSE when no true finite-dimensional model exists. BIC is consistent: it selects the true model with probability $\to 1$ as $n \to \infty$ when the true model is finite-dimensional and in the candidate set. BIC's heavier penalty $p\log n$ (vs. AIC's $2p$) favors sparser models; for $n > e^2 \approx 7.4$, $\log n > 2$ so BIC always penalizes more than AIC.

6.22.2 Cross-Validation

Definition · $k$-Fold Cross-Validation and LOOCV

Partition the $n$ observations into $k$ folds of approximately equal size. For each fold $j = 1, \ldots, k$: fit the model on the $k-1$ remaining folds; predict the held-out fold $j$. The $k$-fold CV estimate of test MSE is: $$\text{CV}_{(k)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i - \hat{f}^{-\kappa(i)}(x_i))^2,$$ where $\hat{f}^{-\kappa(i)}$ is the model fit omitting the fold containing observation $i$. Setting $k = n$ gives leave-one-out CV (LOOCV). For OLS, LOOCV has the remarkable closed-form expression: $$\text{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n\!\left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^{\!2},$$ computed from the full-data fit without refitting $n$ times. The denominator $1 - h_{ii}$ inflates the residual to account for the leverage of observation $i$: high-leverage points have smaller raw residuals but predict worse when left out.

Proof · LOOCV Shortcut for OLS

Let $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)}$ be OLS fit without observation $i$. By the Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury formula applied to rank-1 updates: $$\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)} = \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} - (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}.$$ The leave-one-out residual is: $$y_i - \mathbf{x}_i^\top\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{(i)} = e_i + \mathbf{x}_i^\top(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}_i\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = e_i + \frac{h_{ii}e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}.$$ Squaring and averaging over $i$ gives the LOOCV formula. □

Figure 6.13. Model selection for a simulated dataset ($n=60$) where the true model has 3 non-zero predictors out of 15 candidates. Left: AIC (blue, circles) and BIC (gold, squares) versus number of predictors $k$. Both decrease sharply up to $k=3$ then level off or increase. BIC selects $k=3$ (true model); AIC selects $k=4$ (one extra predictor). Dashed vertical lines mark each criterion's minimum. Right: Adjusted $R^2$ increases to the true model size and plateaus — less decisive than AIC/BIC but consistent in direction.

6.22.3 Stepwise Selection and Its Dangers

Example · Forward Stepwise Selection

Algorithm: Start with the intercept-only model. At each step, add the predictor that most improves AIC (or BIC, or adjusted $R^2$). Continue until no predictor improves the criterion. The selected model is the one at the stopping step.

Limitations: (1) Greedy — does not guarantee finding the globally best model by the criterion (e.g., adding $X_1$ might prevent the algorithm from finding that $X_2 + X_3$ is better, even though neither $X_2$ nor $X_3$ alone helps). (2) Inflated statistics — reported $R^2$, $p$-values, and AIC are optimistically biased because many models were implicitly compared. The "best" model is selected partly by chance. (3) Unstable — small changes in data can lead to very different selected models, especially when predictors are correlated. Bootstrap resampling of the selection procedure reveals this instability: run stepwise on 100 bootstrap samples and see which predictors appear in $>50\%$ of selected models.

Warning · Data Snooping and Invalid Inference After Model Selection

If you use the same data to both select a model and report its statistics (coefficients, $p$-values, $R^2$), all reported statistics are optimistically biased — sometimes severely so. The selected model appears to fit better than it truly does, $p$-values are too small, and confidence intervals are too narrow. The correct procedures are: (1) split data into training and test sets before any modeling; select and fit the model on training data; report performance on the test set (used only once). (2) Use nested cross-validation: outer loop estimates generalization error; inner loop selects the model within each outer fold. Both are computationally expensive but the only way to obtain valid, unbiased assessments.

💡
Key insight: AIC and BIC address different questions. AIC asks "which model predicts best?" — choose it when you want the model that minimizes future prediction error regardless of whether it is the "true" model. BIC asks "which model is most likely to be the true data-generating mechanism?" — choose it for scientific inference about structure. When the true model exists and is finite-dimensional, BIC is asymptotically correct; when it does not (all models are wrong, some useful), AIC minimizes test error.

✦ Chapter Summary

This chapter developed linear regression from its deepest probabilistic foundations through every major methodological concern in modern applied practice. Part I established that regression is the problem of estimating a conditional expectation — the universally optimal predictor under squared error loss — and derived the bias–variance decomposition that governs all statistical learning. Part II developed simple linear regression in full depth: closed-form OLS estimators, their unbiasedness and minimum variance, the Gauss–Markov theorem, exact inference under normality, the four diagnostic plots, $R^2$ and its pitfalls. Part III extended all results to multiple regression via matrix notation, revealing partial regression coefficients through the Frisch–Waugh lens, deriving the multivariate Gauss–Markov theorem, and developing global and partial $F$-tests. Part IV treated the full diagnostic and robustness toolkit: the LINE assumption hierarchy, VIF and condition number for multicollinearity, Cook's distance and DFBETAS for influence, and M-estimators for robustness to outliers. Part V extended the framework through log and Box–Cox transformations, weighted and generalized least squares, polynomial and spline basis expansions, LOESS, indicator coding, and interaction terms. Part VI completed the chapter with the theory of overfitting, the bias–variance trade-off as an operational guide, and the model selection toolkit of AIC, BIC, cross-validation, and regularization paths.

REGRESSION FUNCTION

$\mu(x)=\mathbb{E}[Y|X=x]$ uniquely minimizes MSE over all measurable predictors. Irreducible error $\mathbb{E}[\text{Var}(Y|X)]$ cannot be reduced by any estimator. Linear regression approximates $\mu(x)\approx\beta_0+\beta_1 x$, correct when linear or as a local approximation. Every supervised learning method estimates this function.

BIAS–VARIANCE DECOMPOSITION

$\text{MSE}_{\text{te}}=\sigma^2+\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f})+\text{Var}(\hat{f})$. Simple models: high bias, low variance (underfitting). Complex models: low bias, high variance (overfitting). Regularization introduces bias to reduce variance. Optimal complexity minimizes total expected test error, not training error.

OLS AS PROJECTION

$\hat{\mathbf{y}}=\mathbf{H}\mathbf{y}$ projects $\mathbf{y}$ onto $\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$; $\mathbf{H}=\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$ (idempotent, symmetric, tr$=p$). Residual $\mathbf{e}\perp\mathcal{C}(\mathbf{X})$: $\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{e}=\mathbf{0}$. ANOVA identity SST=SSR+SSE is Pythagoras in $\mathbb{R}^n$. QR decomposition preferred numerically.

OLS ESTIMATORS

$\hat{\beta}_1=S_{xy}/S_{xx}=r_{xy}(s_y/s_x)$; $\hat{\beta}_0=\bar{y}-\hat{\beta}_1\bar{x}$; SSE$=S_{yy}-\hat{\beta}_1 S_{xy}$; $s^2=\text{SSE}/(n-p)$ unbiased for $\sigma^2$. Regression to the mean: standardized slope $=r_{xy}\leq1$. Multiple: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}=(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$; $\hat{\beta}_j$ is partial coefficient (Frisch–Waugh).

GAUSS–MARKOV

Under $(0,\sigma^2)$ uncorrelated errors (no normality), OLS is BLUE. Proof: any alternative linear unbiased estimator $\tilde{\beta}_1=\sum(c_i+d_i)y_i$ has $\text{Var}(\tilde{\beta}_1)=\sigma^2/S_{xx}+\sigma^2\sum d_i^2\geq\sigma^2/S_{xx}$. Multivariate: $\text{Cov}(\tilde{\boldsymbol{\beta}})-\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}})\succeq\mathbf{0}$. Normality needed only for exact $t/F$.

INFERENCE

Under normality: $(\hat{\beta}_j-\beta_j)/(s\sqrt{c_{jj}})\sim t_{n-p}$. CI: $\hat{\beta}_j\pm t^*\cdot s\sqrt{c_{jj}}$. PI at $\mathbf{x}^*$: adds factor $\sqrt{1+\mathbf{x}^{*\top}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{x}^*}$ (does not shrink to zero as $n\to\infty$). Global $F$: MSR/MSE $\sim F_{p-1,n-p}$. Partial $F$: $(\text{SSE}_R-\text{SSE}_F)/(q\cdot\text{MSE}_F)\sim F_{q,n-p}$.

DIAGNOSTICS

Four plots check LINE assumptions. Use $r_i=e_i/(s\sqrt{1-h_{ii}})$ (standardized), not raw $e_i$. Leverage: $h_{ii}=[\mathbf{H}]_{ii}$; flag $>2p/n$. Cook's $D_i=r_i^2 h_{ii}/[p(1-h_{ii})]$; flag $>4/n$. DFBETAS: per-coefficient influence; flag $>2/\sqrt{n}$. Durbin-Watson: autocorrelation $\approx2(1-\hat{\rho})$.

MULTICOLLINEARITY

VIF$_j=1/(1-R_j^2)$: flag $>10$. Condition number $\kappa(\mathbf{X})$: flag $>100$. Effect: inflated SE, sign instability, but predictions in-range still reliable. Remedies: remove predictors, PCR, ridge ($\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda=(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$), collect more data.

TRANSFORMATIONS & EXTENSIONS

Box–Cox $y^{(\lambda)}=(y^\lambda-1)/\lambda$ ($\lambda=0$: log). Log-linear slope = semi-elasticity. VST: Poisson$\to\sqrt{Y}$, lognormal$\to\log Y$. WLS: weights $w_i=1/\sigma_i^2$. GLS (Aitken): $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\Omega=(\mathbf{X}^\top\Omega^{-1}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\Omega^{-1}\mathbf{y}$ is BLUE under $\text{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon})=\sigma^2\Omega$.

BASIS EXPANSIONS & CATEGORICALS

Polynomial: $\phi_k(x)=x^k$; Runge's phenomenon at high degree. Natural cubic splines: $K+2$ parameters, smooth, stable. LOESS: local polynomial, nonparametric. Indicator coding: $K-1$ dummies, reference category. Interactions: $D\times X$ gives group-specific slopes; center continuous variables before forming products.

MODEL SELECTION

AIC$=n\log(\text{SSE}/n)+2p$ (efficient); BIC$=n\log(\text{SSE}/n)+p\log n$ (consistent). LOOCV$=n^{-1}\sum(e_i/(1-h_{ii}))^2$ (OLS closed form). $k$-fold CV more stable than LOOCV. Ridge: $\lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2$ penalty, biased but lower variance. Lasso: $\lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|_1$, sparse. Never report statistics from model-selected data without holdout set.

ROBUST REGRESSION

OLS breakdown point: $1/n\to0$. Huber M-estimator: $\rho_k(u)=u^2/2$ if $|u|\leq k$, else $k|u|-k^2/2$; 95% efficiency under normality. Solved by IRLS; down-weights large residuals. LMS/RANSAC: 50% breakdown point. Always verify suspicious influential points before deleting; report both with and without.

Exercises

Exercise 6.1  ·  Projection and Optimality
Geometry of Least Squares: From Pythagoras to Numerical Stability

Let $\mathbf{X}$ be $n \times p$ of rank $p$ and $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top$.

  1. Prove the ANOVA identity from Pythagoras. Show $\|\mathbf{v}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{H}\mathbf{v}\|^2 + \|(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H})\mathbf{v}\|^2$ for any $\mathbf{v}$. Apply with $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{y} - \bar{y}\mathbf{1}$ to derive SST = SSR + SSE, stating precisely where the intercept assumption enters.
  2. QR decomposition. If $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{R}$ (thin QR, $\mathbf{Q}^\top\mathbf{Q} = \mathbf{I}_p$), show $\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{Q}^\top$ and $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{Q}^\top\mathbf{y}$. Explain why this is preferable to the normal equations for a design matrix with condition number $\kappa = 500$.
  3. Leverage bounds. Prove $1/n \leq h_{ii} \leq 1$ when $\mathbf{X}$ contains a column of ones. Find the exact form of $h_{ii}$ in simple linear regression and identify which observation has the smallest leverage.
  4. Data science connection. In a ridge regression $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$, derive the effective hat matrix $\mathbf{H}_\lambda$ and show tr$(\mathbf{H}_\lambda) = \sum_j d_j^2/(d_j^2+\lambda)$, where $d_j$ are the singular values of $\mathbf{X}$. Interpret this as "effective degrees of freedom" and explain what happens as $\lambda\to0$ and $\lambda\to\infty$.
Exercise 6.2  ·  Gauss–Markov and Inference
BLUE Properties, Exact Intervals, and Robustness in a Dose-Response Study

A pharmacologist fits simple linear regression of log-response $y$ on dose $x$ using $n = 20$ observations at $x_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, 20\}$ mg. Results: $\hat{\beta}_0 = 0.30$, $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.12$, $s = 0.18$, $\bar{x} = 10.5$, $S_{xx} = 665$.

  1. Hypothesis test. Test $H_0: \beta_1 = 0$ vs $H_1: \beta_1 > 0$ at $\alpha = 0.01$. Compute the $t$-statistic, compare to the exact critical value $t_{18, 0.01}$, and state the scientific conclusion regarding a positive dose-response relationship.
  2. Confidence and prediction intervals. Construct 95% CI for $\mathbb{E}[Y|X=15]$ and 95% PI for a new single measurement at $X=15$. Verify the PI is wider and explain what sources of uncertainty each interval quantifies.
  3. Alternative estimator. Consider $\tilde{\beta}_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{20} w_i y_i$ with $w_i = (2i-1)/(20^2)$ (linearly increasing weights). Show $\tilde{\beta}_1$ is linear in $\{y_i\}$, verify whether it is unbiased for $\beta_1$, and if so compute $\text{Var}(\tilde{\beta}_1)/\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1)$. Interpret the ratio in terms of the Gauss–Markov theorem.
  4. Heteroscedasticity. Suppose now $\text{Var}(\varepsilon_i) = \sigma^2 x_i$ (variance proportional to dose). Derive the WLS estimator for this case, compute its variance, and compare to $\text{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{OLS}}) = \sigma^2/S_{xx}$. Which is smaller, and why does the Gauss–Markov theorem not guarantee OLS is BLUE here?
Exercise 6.3  ·  Diagnostics and Influence
Identifying and Quantifying Assumption Violations in Cross-Country Economic Data

A regression of log-GDP per capita ($Y$) on schooling years ($X_1$) and trade openness ($X_2$) across $n = 80$ countries gives $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (4.2, 0.38, 0.61)^\top$, $s = 0.55$, $\text{SSE} = 23.1$. Country A: $h_{AA} = 0.38$, $e_A = -1.82$. Country B: $h_{BB} = 0.08$, $e_B = 0.22$.

  1. Cook's distance. Compute standardized residuals $r_A$, $r_B$ and Cook's distances $D_A$, $D_B$ using $D_i = r_i^2 h_{ii}/[p(1-h_{ii})]$ with $p=3$. Apply both the $4/n$ and $D_i > 1$ thresholds. Which country is more influential and why?
  2. Heteroscedasticity diagnosis. The scale-location plot shows a positive slope across the fitted value range. (i) What does this pattern indicate? (ii) Propose and justify a transformation of $Y$ to remedy it. (iii) After applying $\log\log Y$, the scale-location is still slightly positive. What does this suggest?
  3. Non-normality. The Q–Q plot shows heavy tails (S-shaped departure at both ends). Name two specific consequences for (i) the validity of individual $t$-tests and (ii) the coverage of prediction intervals. Describe how a bootstrap with $B = 1000$ resamples would construct a confidence interval for $\beta_1$ that is robust to non-normality.
  4. Outlier test. Using the approximation $t_A \approx r_A\sqrt{(n-p-1)/(n-p-r_A^2)}$ for the externally studentized residual, test whether Country A is a significant outlier at the Bonferroni-corrected level $\alpha/n = 0.05/80$. State the null hypothesis, test statistic distribution, and conclusion.
Exercise 6.4  ·  Transformations and Basis Expansions
Nonlinear Effects, Splines, and Interaction Terms in Environmental Pollution Modeling

Air quality data: PM2.5 ($Y$, $\mu$g/m$^3$) at $n = 200$ monitoring stations, modeled using temperature $X_1$ (°C), wind speed $X_2$ (m/s), and season (winter = reference, summer = $D_{\text{su}}$, fall = $D_{\text{fa}}$, spring = $D_{\text{sp}}$).

  1. Log transform. Box–Cox analysis yields $\hat{\lambda} = 0.08$ (compatible with $\lambda = 0$). Fit $\log Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \beta_3 D_{\text{su}} + \beta_4 D_{\text{fa}} + \beta_5 D_{\text{sp}} + \varepsilon$. Interpret $\hat{\beta}_3 = -0.42$: by how much does median PM2.5 differ between summer and winter, holding temperature and wind speed constant?
  2. Nonlinear temperature effect. After log-transform, the residuals vs. $X_1$ plot shows a concave-down curve. You add a quadratic term $X_1^2$. Construct the full design matrix for one observation $(x_1 = 25, x_2 = 3, \text{summer})$ in this model. Show that the partial $F$-test for $H_0: \beta_6 = 0$ (quadratic term) is equivalent to the $t$-test on $\hat{\beta}_6$.
  3. Multicollinearity from polynomials. VIF for $X_1$ in the $\{X_1, X_1^2\}$ model is 45. Show that centering — replacing $X_1$ with $\tilde{X}_1 = X_1 - \bar{X}_1$ — reduces the correlation between $\tilde{X}_1$ and $\tilde{X}_1^2$ to zero when $\bar{X}_1 = 0$. Compute VIF analytically for the two-predictor centered case.
  4. Spline vs. polynomial. Compare fitting a natural cubic spline with 4 interior knots (6 free parameters) to a degree-5 polynomial (6 free parameters) on the temperature effect. For each, state: (i) number of parameters, (ii) behavior outside the observed temperature range, (iii) risk of numerical instability, (iv) interpretability of individual coefficients. Which would you recommend for a regulatory report that must be defended in public?
Exercise 6.5  ·  Model Selection and Regularization
AIC, BIC, Cross-Validation, and Ridge Regression in a High-Dimensional Genomics Study

Gene expression: $n = 120$ patients, $p_{\text{cand}} = 50$ candidate predictors. Full 50-predictor OLS gives $R^2 = 0.91$, $\text{SSE}_{\text{full}} = 820$. An 8-predictor selected model gives $\text{SSE}_8 = 940$. $\text{SST} = 9111$.

  1. Information criteria comparison. Compute AIC and BIC for both the 8-predictor and 50-predictor models (remember $p$ includes the intercept). Show analytically why BIC penalizes additional parameters more heavily than AIC for this sample size, and state which model each criterion selects.
  2. LOOCV closed form. Derive the LOOCV formula $\text{LOOCV} = n^{-1}\sum(e_i/(1-h_{ii}))^2$ for OLS starting from the Sherman-Morrison-Woodbury rank-1 update. Explain why observations with $h_{ii}$ close to 1 can make LOOCV numerically unreliable and what a computationally safer alternative is.
  3. Ridge regression. Derive $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda = (\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{y}$ by minimizing $\|\mathbf{y}-\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2+\lambda\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2$. Show (i) $\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda$ is biased for $\boldsymbol{\beta}$; (ii) $\text{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_\lambda) = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}+\lambda\mathbf{I})^{-1}$; (iii) there exists a $\lambda^* > 0$ such that $\text{MSE}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\lambda^*}) < \text{MSE}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_{\text{OLS}})$ whenever $\|\boldsymbol{\beta}\|^2 < p\sigma^2 / d_{\min}^2$ (where $d_{\min}$ is the smallest singular value of $\mathbf{X}$).
  4. Valid model evaluation. The researcher reports $R^2_{\text{test}} = 0.89$ on the same data used to select and fit the 8-predictor model. Explain the fundamental flaw. Design a complete nested cross-validation protocol (outer 10-fold, inner 5-fold) that correctly estimates generalization error while simultaneously selecting the number of predictors via BIC in each inner fold. Specify what is computed in each fold and what the final reported statistic represents.
Chapter 6  ·  Statistical Inference  ·  Data Science Foundations  ·  Parts I–VI  ·  Sections 1–22
